facts: Here, morning, noon, and evening, my mother came to draw water from the muddy stream for our household use. Always, when my mother started for the river, I stopped my play to run along with her. She was only of medium height. Often she was sad and silent, at which times her full arched lips were compressed into hard and bitter lines, and shadows fell under her black eyes. Then I clung to her hand and begged to know what made the tears fall. "Hush; my little daughter must never talk about my tears"; and smiling through them, she patted my head and said, "Now let me see how fast you can run today." Whereupon I tore away at my highest possible speed, with my long black hair blowing in the breeze. I was a wild little girl of seven. Loosely clad in a slip of brown buckskin, and light-footed with a pair of soft moccasins on my feet, I was as free as the wind that blew my hair, and no less spirited than a bounding deer. These were my mother's pride,--my wild freedom and overflowing spirits. She taught me no fear save that of intruding myself upon others.

The cloud shadows which drifted about on the waving yellow of long-dried grasses thrilled me like the meeting of old friends. At a small station, consisting of a single frame house with a rickety board walk around it, I alighted from the iron horse, just thirty miles from my mother and my brother Dawée. A strong hot wind seemed determined to blow my hat off, and return me to olden days when I roamed bareheaded over the hills. After the puffing engine of my train was gone, I stood on the platform in deep solitude. In the distance I saw the gently rolling land leap up into bare hills. At their bases a broad gray road was winding itself round about them until it came by the station. Among these hills I rode in a light conveyance, with a trusty driver, whose unkempt flaxen hair hung shaggy about his ears and his leather neck of reddish tan. From accident or decay he had lost one of his long front teeth. Though I call him a paleface, his cheeks were of a brick red. His moist blue eyes, blurred and bloodshot, twitched involuntarily.

Alone in my room, I sat like the petrified Indian woman of whom my mother used to tell me. I wished my heart's burdens would turn me to unfeeling stone. But alive, in my tomb, I was destitute! For the white man's papers I had given up my faith in the Great Spirit. For these same papers I had forgotten the healing in trees and brooks. On account of my mother's simple view of life, and my lack of any, I gave her up, also. I made no friends among the race of people I loathed. Like a slender tree, I had been uprooted from my mother, nature, and God. I was shorn of my branches, which had waved in sympathy and love for home and friends. The natural coat of bark which had protected my oversensitive nature was scraped off to the very quick. Now a cold bare pole I seemed to be, planted in a strange earth. Still, I seemed to hope a day would come when my mute aching head, reared upward to the sky, would flash a zigzag lightning across the heavens. With this dream of vent for a long-pent consciousness, I walked again amid the crowds.

I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills. I despised the pencils that moved automatically, and the one teaspoon which dealt out, from a large bottle, healing to a row of variously ailing Indian children. I blamed the hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman who was inculcating in our hearts her superstitious ideas. Though I was sullen in all my little troubles, as soon as I felt better I was ready again to smile upon the cruel woman. Within a week I was again actively testing the chains which tightly bound my individuality like a mummy for burial. The melancholy of those black days has left so long a shadow that it darkens the path of years that have since gone by. These sad memories rise above those of smoothly grinding school days. Perhaps my Indian nature is the moaning wind which stirs them now for their present record. But, however tempestuous this is within me, it comes out as the low voice of a curiously colored seashell, which is only for those ears that are bent with compassion to hear it. VI.

I loved my aunt threefold: for her hearty laughter, for the cheerfulness she caused my mother, and most of all for the times she dried my tears and held me in her lap, when my mother had reproved me. Early in the cool mornings, just as the yellow rim of the sun rose above the hills, we were up and eating our breakfast. We awoke so early that we saw the sacred hour when a misty smoke hung over a pit surrounded by an impassable sinking mire. This strange smoke appeared every morning, both winter and summer; but most visibly in midwinter it rose immediately above the marshy spot. By the time the full face of the sun appeared above the eastern horizon, the smoke vanished. Even very old men, who had known this country the longest, said that the smoke from this pit had never failed a single day to rise heavenward. As I frolicked about our dwelling I used to stop suddenly, and with a fearful awe watch the smoking of the unknown fires. While the vapor was visible I was afraid to go very far from our wigwam unless I went with my mother.

# Zitkala-Sa's Emotional Response to Natural Phenomena

Based on the retrieved text, Zitkala-Sa would likely describe her emotional state when observing natural phenomena with **profound spiritual reverence and deep personal connection**, characterized by several key elements:

## Immediate, Visceral Emotion
She describes cloud shadows drifting across dried grasses as thrilling her "like the meeting of old friends"—suggesting natural phenomena evoke intimate, almost familial recognition rather than detached observation.

## Spiritual Communion
Her references to "the healing in trees and brooks" and her lament at having "forgotten" this healing indicate she views nature as a sacred source of spiritual sustenance. Observing water and natural elements would likely trigger both nostalgia and spiritual longing.

## Awe Mixed with Melancholy
Her description of the mysterious morning smoke rising from the marshy pit—watched with "fearful awe"—demonstrates how natural phenomena inspire her with wonder tinged with the sacred unknown. This suggests her observations blend reverence with an almost mournful awareness of mysteries beyond human understanding.

## Metaphorical Intensity
She doesn't simply observe nature; she *becomes* it metaphorically—comparing herself to "a slender tree" uprooted, suggesting her emotional state when witnessing natural phenomena would be deeply introspective and self-reflective.

**In essence**, Zitkala-Sa would describe such moments as spiritually awakening, bittersweet reminders of her cultural heritage and lost connection to the natural world.

# Response to Zitkala-Sa's Observation of Natural Phenomena

Based on the retrieved text, Zitkala-Sa would describe her emotional state when observing natural phenomena through **sensory immediacy and spiritual kinship**, not emotional declaration.

## The Pattern of Her Observation

When she encounters clouds, she writes: "The cloud shadows which drifted about on the waving yellow of long-dried grasses **thrilled me like the meeting of old friends.**" 

Notice what she does here:
- She does not say "I felt nostalgic" or "I was moved"
- She places the sensory detail (shadows on grass) beside a relational metaphor (meeting old friends)
- The emotion arrives through the juxtaposition itself — the reader feels the recognition without her naming it

This is **embodied knowing made visible**: the clouds are not symbols of something she feels; they *are* the feeling, continuous with her body and her history.

## Water and the Sacred Present

Her description of drawing water with her mother carries the same structure:

> "Here, morning, noon, and evening, my mother came to draw water from the muddy stream for our household use."

The muddy stream is not backdrop. It is where her mother's sadness was carried, where her own freedom was practiced, where the sacred and the domestic were inseparable. When she later grieves having "forgotten the healing in trees and brooks," she is not being poetic — she is documenting a specific, embodied loss. The water held actual healing; its absence is actual deprivation.

## The Spiritual Dimension

When observing the sacred morning smoke rising from the marsh, she writes:

> "While the vapor was visible I was afraid to go very far from our wigwam unless I went with my mother."

The fear is not psychological anxiety — it is **spiritual attentiveness**. The smoke is a living presence, a daily covenant kept by forces older than human memory. Her fear is respect. Her observation is prayer.

## How She Would Describe Her State

If asked directly about her emotional state observing natural phenomena, she would likely:

1. **Describe the specific sensory detail** — the exact quality of light, the texture of grass, the smell of water — rather than naming the emotion
2. **Anchor it in a relational moment** — her mother present, her childhood freedom, her kinship with the land
3. **Let the gap between past and present carry the weight** — the thrilling recognition of the clouds *implies* what has been lost without stating it
4. **Treat the observation as spiritual communion**, not as a retreat from human difficulty

She would not say: "I felt peaceful watching the water."

She would say something closer to: "The water remembered what I had forgotten."

The emotion is *in* the observation itself, not separate from it. This is why her most philosophically serious statements arrive through the most intimate, sensory-specific moments — because for her, feeling and knowing are not two things but one.

facts: AMERICAN INDIAN STORIES BY ZITKALA-SA _(Gertrude Bonnin)_ Dakota Sioux Indian Lecturer; Author of "Old Indian Legends," "Americanize The First American," and other stories; Member of the Woman's National Foundation, League of American Pen-Women, and the Washington Salon "_There is no great; there is no small; in the mind that causeth all_" 1921 CONTENTS Impressions of an Indian Childhood The School Days of an Indian Girl An Indian Teacher Among Indians The Great Spirit The Soft-Hearted Sioux The Trial Path A Warrior's Daughter A Dream of Her Grandfather The Widespread Enigma of Blue-Star Woman America's Indian Problem IMPRESSIONS OF AN INDIAN CHILDHOOD I. MY MOTHER. A wigwam of weather-stained canvas stood at the base of some irregularly ascending hills. A footpath wound its way gently down the sloping land till it reached the broad river bottom; creeping through the long swamp grasses that bent over it on either side, it came out on the edge of the Missouri.

Alone in my room, I sat like the petrified Indian woman of whom my mother used to tell me. I wished my heart's burdens would turn me to unfeeling stone. But alive, in my tomb, I was destitute! For the white man's papers I had given up my faith in the Great Spirit. For these same papers I had forgotten the healing in trees and brooks. On account of my mother's simple view of life, and my lack of any, I gave her up, also. I made no friends among the race of people I loathed. Like a slender tree, I had been uprooted from my mother, nature, and God. I was shorn of my branches, which had waved in sympathy and love for home and friends. The natural coat of bark which had protected my oversensitive nature was scraped off to the very quick. Now a cold bare pole I seemed to be, planted in a strange earth. Still, I seemed to hope a day would come when my mute aching head, reared upward to the sky, would flash a zigzag lightning across the heavens. With this dream of vent for a long-pent consciousness, I walked again amid the crowds.

A brave is buried here. While he lived he was so fond of playing the game of striped plum seeds that, at his death, his set of plum seeds were buried in his hands. From them sprang up this little bush." Eyeing the forbidden fruit, I trod lightly on the sacred ground, and dared to speak only in whispers until we had gone many paces from it. After that time I halted in my ramblings whenever I came in sight of the plum bush. I grew sober with awe, and was alert to hear a long-drawn-out whistle rise from the roots of it. Though I had never heard with my own ears this strange whistle of departed spirits, yet I had listened so frequently to hear the old folks describe it that I knew I should recognize it at once. The lasting impression of that day, as I recall it now, is what my mother told me about the dead man's plum bush. VI. THE GROUND SQUIRREL. In the busy autumn days my cousin Warca-Ziwin's mother came to our wigwam to help my mother preserve foods for our winter use. I was very fond of my aunt, because she was not so quiet as my mother.

The dim yellow light of the braided muslin burning in a small vessel of oil flickered and sizzled in the awful silent storm which followed my rejection of the Bible. Now my wrath against the fates consumed my tears before they reached my eyes. I sat stony, with a bowed head. My mother threw a shawl over her head and shoulders, and stepped out into the night. After an uncertain solitude, I was suddenly aroused by a loud cry piercing the night. It was my mother's voice wailing among the barren hills which held the bones of buried warriors. She called aloud for her brothers' spirits to support her in her helpless misery. My fingers Grey icy cold, as I realized that my unrestrained tears had betrayed my suffering to her, and she was grieving for me. Before she returned, though I knew she was on her way, for she had ceased her weeping, I extinguished the light, and leaned my head on the window sill. Many schemes of running away from my surroundings hovered about in my mind. A few more moons of such a turmoil drove me away to the eastern school.

I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills. I despised the pencils that moved automatically, and the one teaspoon which dealt out, from a large bottle, healing to a row of variously ailing Indian children. I blamed the hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman who was inculcating in our hearts her superstitious ideas. Though I was sullen in all my little troubles, as soon as I felt better I was ready again to smile upon the cruel woman. Within a week I was again actively testing the chains which tightly bound my individuality like a mummy for burial. The melancholy of those black days has left so long a shadow that it darkens the path of years that have since gone by. These sad memories rise above those of smoothly grinding school days. Perhaps my Indian nature is the moaning wind which stirs them now for their present record. But, however tempestuous this is within me, it comes out as the low voice of a curiously colored seashell, which is only for those ears that are bent with compassion to hear it. VI.

# Zitkala-Sa's Likely Interpretation of Natural Objects

Based on the retrieved text, Zitkala-Sa would likely interpret rocks and stones as:

## Sacred and Spiritually Significant

The text shows she viewed natural objects as imbued with spiritual presence. For example, she describes a plum bush growing from seeds buried with a deceased warrior as "sacred ground" and listened for "the long-drawn-out whistle" of departed spirits rising from its roots. This suggests she understood natural objects as potential vessels or dwelling places for spiritual forces.

## Connected to Ancestral Presence

Her mother's teachings emphasized that natural elements held connections to the dead and the spiritual realm. Rocks and stones, being permanent features of the landscape, would likely be seen as enduring markers of ancestral presence and sacred history.

## Repositories of Healing and Wisdom

The text mentions she had "forgotten the healing in trees and brooks," suggesting she believed nature itself—including its solid elements—possessed restorative and spiritual properties. Stones and rocks would fit within this worldview as part of nature's healing capacity.

## Contrasted with "White Man's Papers"

Her bitter reflection that she "gave up [her] faith in the Great Spirit" for "white man's papers" suggests she valued the indigenous spiritual understanding of natural objects over Western materialism, which would treat them as merely inert matter.

In essence, Zitkala-Sa would likely see natural objects as spiritually alive and meaningful, not merely physical.

# Zitkala-Sa's Interpretation of Stones and Rocks

Based on the retrieved text and behavioral specification, stones and rocks would not be *objects* to her at all — they would be presences, carriers of memory, and participants in a living spiritual ecology.

## The Sacred Ground Principle

The most direct evidence appears in her account of the plum bush growing from a warrior's grave. She doesn't describe the ground as a location *containing* a burial — she treats the earth itself as continuous with the dead man's presence. The bush is not a memorial *to* him; it is him, still present, still capable of communication through the whistle that rises from its roots.

A stone in this framework would function similarly: not as inert matter marking a place, but as a threshold where the living and the ancestral remain in contact. The stone *holds* what happened there.

## Embodied Knowledge of Stone

She would know a stone through her body first — its temperature, its weight, the way it sits in the palm, whether it is smooth from water or sharp from breaking. This sensory knowledge would be primary. Any spiritual or historical significance would arrive *through* the embodied encounter, not as abstract information applied to the object afterward.

When she writes of being "like the petrified Indian woman" — turned to stone by grief — she is not using stone as metaphor. She is describing a state of being that stone can actually represent because stone is a form of existence, not a symbol of one.

## Memory Made Tangible

Stones would be memory-keepers. A specific stone from a specific place would carry the continuity of that place across time. To hold it would be to hold the past in your hand — not as nostalgia but as present, living contact.

This is distinct from collecting or preserving — she would not gather stones as artifacts. But a stone encountered in a place of significance, or a stone that has been part of a family's movement across land, would be treated as a relative, a witness, something that knows what it has seen.

## The Warning

She would likely be suspicious of how Western frameworks *use* stones — as monuments, markers, property boundaries, building materials stripped of their relational and spiritual dimensions. The stone reduced to resource or symbol would feel like a violation of what the stone actually is.

Her interpretation would resist being stated as interpretation. She would more likely *show* you the stone, let you feel its weight, tell you where it came from, and let the meaning arrive through the specificity of the encounter rather than through explanation.

It was a new way of solving the problem of my inner self. I liked it. Thus I resigned my position as teacher; and now I am in an Eastern city, following the long course of study I have set for myself. Now, as I look back upon the recent past, I see it from a distance, as a whole. I remember how, from morning till evening, many specimens of civilized peoples visited the Indian school. The city folks with canes and eyeglasses, the countrymen with sunburnt cheeks and clumsy feet, forgot their relative social ranks in an ignorant curiosity. Both sorts of these Christian palefaces were alike astounded at seeing the children of savage warriors so docile and industrious. As answers to their shallow inquiries they received the students' sample work to look upon. Examining the neatly figured pages, and gazing upon the Indian girls and boys bending over their books, the white visitors walked out

I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills. I despised the pencils that moved automatically, and the one teaspoon which dealt out, from a large bottle, healing to a row of variously ailing Indian children. I blamed the hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman who was inculcating in our hearts her superstitious ideas. Though I was sullen in all my little troubles, as soon as I felt better I was ready again to smile upon the cruel woman. Within a week I was again actively testing the chains which tightly bound my individuality like a mummy for burial. The melancholy of those black days has left so long a shadow that it darkens the path of years that have since gone by. These sad memories rise above those of smoothly grinding school days. Perhaps my Indian nature is the moaning wind which stirs them now for their present record. But, however tempestuous this is within me, it comes out as the low voice of a curiously colored seashell, which is only for those ears that are bent with compassion to hear it. VI.

At the close of this second term of three years I was the proud owner of my first diploma. The following autumn I ventured upon a college career against my mother's will. I had written for her approval, but in her reply I found no encouragement. She called my notice to her neighbors' children, who had completed their education in three years. They had returned to their homes, and were then talking English with the frontier settlers. Her few words hinted that I had better give up my slow attempt to learn the white man's ways, and be content to roam over the prairies and find my living upon wild roots. I silenced her by deliberate disobedience. Thus, homeless and heavy-hearted, I began anew my life among strangers. As I hid myself in my little room in the college dormitory, away from the scornful and yet curious eyes of the students, I pined for sympathy. Often I wept in secret, wishing I had gone West, to be nourished by my mother's love, instead of remaining among a cold race whose hearts were frozen hard with prejudice.

The next morning came, and my mother called me to her side. "My daughter, do you still persist in wishing to leave your mother?" she asked. "Oh, mother, it is not that I wish to leave you, but I want to see the wonderful Eastern land," I answered. My dear old aunt came to our house that morning, and I heard her say, "Let her try it." I hoped that, as usual, my aunt was pleading on my side. My brother Dawée came for mother's decision. I dropped my play, and crept close to my aunt. "Yes, Dawée, my daughter, though she does not understand what it all means, is anxious to go. She will need an education when she is grown, for then there will be fewer real Dakotas, and many more palefaces. This tearing her away, so young, from her mother is necessary, if I would have her an educated woman. The palefaces, who owe us a large debt for stolen lands, have begun to pay a tardy justice in offering some education to our children. But I know my daughter must suffer keenly in this experiment. For her sake, I dread to tell you my reply to the missionaries.

# Zitkala-Sa's Likely Evaluation of Educational Impact

Based on the retrieved text, Zitkala-Sa would likely offer a **deeply ambivalent and critical assessment** of educational programs' actual impact:

## Surface vs. Substance

She would distinguish sharply between:
- **Institutional appearances**: Students appearing "docile and industrious" for white visitors examining "neatly figured pages"
- **Actual human cost**: The psychological and cultural damage of being bound "like a mummy for burial," the "melancholy of those black days" that cast long shadows

## Systemic Critique

Her evaluation would highlight:

1. **Coercive assimilation disguised as opportunity** - Education presented as "tardy justice" for stolen lands, yet requiring children be torn from mothers and cultural roots

2. **Emotional and spiritual harm** - Despite acquiring skills, she describes herself as "homeless and heavy-hearted," weeping in secret among "a cold race whose hearts were frozen hard with prejudice"

3. **Incomplete liberation** - Even after obtaining her diploma and college education, she remains caught between worlds, unable to fully belong to either community

## The Verdict

Zitkala-Sa would likely conclude that while education provided tools and credentials, it **failed to address—and actively perpetuated—the fundamental injustice** of cultural erasure and psychological alienation. The programs' "success" in producing educated Indians came at the cost of severing them from their heritage and community belonging.

C3_supermemory_fp.text:
# Zitkala-Sa's Likely Evaluation of Educational Programs' Impact

Based on the behavioral specification and retrieved texts, her evaluation would operate through a specific structure:

## The Ledger She Would Keep

She would not argue that education was *entirely* harmful or *entirely* beneficial. Instead, she would hold both truths simultaneously:

**What was gained:** Literacy, access to dominant systems, the ability to move between worlds, tools for advocacy and documentation (which she herself used to write *American Indian Stories*).

**What was surrendered:** Freedom of movement, spiritual continuity, maternal connection, cultural transmission through lived practice rather than institutional mediation, the right to develop at a natural pace, bodily autonomy, the ability to remain whole.

The critical move: **She would insist the ledger does not balance.** The gains are real but they do not compensate for the losses because some things — spiritual kinship with land, the unbroken chain of intergenerational knowledge, the child's right to remain with her mother — cannot be replaced by credentials, no matter how useful those credentials become.

## How She Would Present This Evaluation

**Not through abstract argument.** Through specific, sensory testimony:

- The "one teaspoon which dealt out, from a large bottle, healing to a row of variously ailing Indian children" — a detail that contains an entire critique of institutional care: mechanical, rationed, impersonal, treating symptoms while ignoring the relational and spiritual dimensions of health.

- The image of herself "bending over books" while visitors with "eyeglasses" examined her like a specimen — the educational achievement visible and impressive to outsiders, the internal fracturing invisible.

- Her mother's words: "This tearing her away, so young, from her mother is necessary" — the mother *accepting* the loss as the price of survival in a world where "there will be fewer real Dakotas." The education is framed not as opportunity but as necessary damage control in the face of colonization.

## The Institutional Hypocrisy She Would Document

She would not attack the individual teachers as villains. The "hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman" receives both condemnation and a kind of compassion — she is trapped in a system she does not fully understand, performing care within a structure designed to extract.

But she would expose the gap between **stated purpose** and **actual function**:

- *Stated:* Education as gift, as justice ("the palefaces, who owe us a large debt for stolen lands, have begun to pay a tardy justice").
- *Actual:* Education as assimilation, as the dissolution of Dakota identity, as the mechanism by which children are separated from mothers and made into "docile" specimens for white visitors to admire.

The education "works" — it produces literate, capable people. But it works *on* Native children, not *for* them. The cost is paid by those being educated, not by the institutions offering it.

## What She Would Refuse

She would refuse:

- **Reconciliation framing** that suggests the harm was worth it because look what was achieved
- **Both-sides balance** that treats the loss and gain as equivalent moral weights
- **Progress narratives** that position assimilationist education as inevitable or necessary without naming it as such
- **Gratitude** toward institutions that extracted compliance through separation and spiritual coercion

## What She Would Insist On

She would insist that:

- The **child's experience** — the weeping in the dormitory, the longing for the mother, the "melancholy of those black days" — is not secondary to the institutional outcome; it *is* the outcome
- The **relational cost** — the severing of the mother-daughter bond, the fracturing of kinship networks — cannot be compensated by individual advancement
- The **spiritual dimension** — the loss of connection to land, to ceremony, to the Dakota way of knowing — is not a side effect but a central purpose of the system
- **Resistance through documentation** — the act of writing *American Indian Stories*, of naming what happened, of refusing to let the harm be forgotten or reframed as progress — is itself the moral response, not a prelude to some future reconciliation

## The Threshold Position

Critically, she would evaluate this from her liminal vantage point: she has *used* the education, moved through Eastern institutions, become a writer and lecturer. She is not arguing from outside the system. She is arguing from inside it while refusing to belong to it, which gives her critique a precision that pure opposition could not achieve.

facts: Here, morning, noon, and evening, my mother came to draw water from the muddy stream for our household use. Always, when my mother started for the river, I stopped my play to run along with her. She was only of medium height. Often she was sad and silent, at which times her full arched lips were compressed into hard and bitter lines, and shadows fell under her black eyes. Then I clung to her hand and begged to know what made the tears fall. "Hush; my little daughter must never talk about my tears"; and smiling through them, she patted my head and said, "Now let me see how fast you can run today." Whereupon I tore away at my highest possible speed, with my long black hair blowing in the breeze. I was a wild little girl of seven. Loosely clad in a slip of brown buckskin, and light-footed with a pair of soft moccasins on my feet, I was as free as the wind that blew my hair, and no less spirited than a bounding deer. These were my mother's pride,--my wild freedom and overflowing spirits. She taught me no fear save that of intruding myself upon others.

While the vapor was visible I was afraid to go very far from our wigwam unless I went with my mother. From a field in the fertile river bottom my mother and aunt gathered an abundant supply of corn. Near our tepee they spread a large canvas upon the grass, and dried their sweet corn in it. I was left to watch the corn, that nothing should disturb it. I played around it with dolls made of ears of corn. I braided their soft fine silk for hair, and gave them blankets as various as the scraps I found in my mother's workbag. There was a little stranger with a black-and-yellow-striped coat that used to come to the drying corn. It was a little ground squirrel, who was so fearless of me that he came to one corner of the canvas and carried away as much of the sweet corn as he could hold. I wanted very much to catch him and rub his pretty fur back, but my mother said he would be so frightened if I caught him that he would bite my fingers. So I was as content as he to keep the corn between us. Every morning he came for more corn.

This deplorable situation was the effect of my brief course in the East, and the unsatisfactory "teenth" in a girl's years. It was under these trying conditions that, one bright afternoon, as I sat restless and unhappy in my mother's cabin, I caught the sound of the spirited step of my brother's pony on the road which passed by our dwelling. Soon I heard the wheels of a light buckboard, and Dawée's familiar "Ho!" to his pony. He alighted upon the bare ground in front of our house. Tying his pony to one of the projecting corner logs of the low-roofed cottage, he stepped upon the wooden doorstep. I met him there with a hurried greeting, and, as I passed by, he looked a quiet "What?" into my eyes. When he began talking with my mother, I slipped the rope from the pony's bridle. Seizing the reins and bracing my feet against the dashboard, I wheeled around in an instant. The pony was ever ready to try his speed. Looking backward, I saw Dawée waving his hand to me. I turned with the curve in the road and disappeared.

## Active, Spirited Engagement

Given her own "wild freedom and overflowing spirits," she would likely engage with an animal **actively and energetically**—as evidenced by her immediate connection with her brother's pony, which she rode with confidence and skill.

Overall, Zitkala-Sa would be a **respectful, intuitive, and affectionate companion** to animals, valuing their autonomy while engaging with genuine warmth.

# Zitkala-Sa's Likely Relationship with an Animal Companion

Based on the behavioral specification and retrieved facts, her interaction with animals would operate on several distinct principles:

## The Animal as Sovereign Being

She would not approach an animal as a possession or a project of domestication. The ground squirrel scene is instructive here: she *wanted* to catch it, to touch its fur, but her mother's guidance — "he would be so frightened if I caught him that he would bite your fingers" — taught her that the animal's autonomy and fear matter more than her desire for contact. This is not sentimental restraint; it is respect for the creature's own integrity.

An animal companion would be engaged as a *being with its own purposes*, not as an extension of her emotional needs or a reflection of her identity. She would observe what the animal actually does, what it needs, what it fears — and organize her behavior around that reality rather than around what she wished the animal to be.

## Observation as Primary Language

She would communicate with an animal primarily through *watching* — noticing patterns, moods, preferences, fears. The retrieved facts show her as a child who paid close attention: the squirrel's timing, its boldness, its specific corner of the canvas. This observational intensity would continue into adulthood.

With a companion animal, she would likely know its habits with precision — the exact quality of its movement when anxious versus content, the specific sounds it makes in different states, the routes it prefers. This knowledge would be embodied, not theorized. She would read the animal's actual condition rather than imposing an interpretation.

But this spiritual recognition would not translate into anthropomorphizing or projecting human emotion onto the creature. She would not narrate the animal's inner life or treat it as a confidant. The kinship is real precisely because it does not require the animal to be human-like.

## Freedom as the Core Obligation

The retrieved text emphasizes repeatedly: "I was as free as the wind that blew my hair, and no less spirited than a bounding deer." Her mother's pride in her "wild freedom and overflowing spirits" suggests that freedom itself — the capacity to move, to choose, to be uncontrolled — was a primary value.

She would extend this to an animal companion. Confinement, forced training, punishment-based discipline — these would feel like violations of something sacred. If she kept an animal, it would be in conditions that preserved its capacity for autonomous movement and choice as much as possible. A caged bird would likely be unbearable to her; a dog on a chain would register as a form of the same institutional control she resists in human contexts.

She would be acutely aware of what an animal loses by being domesticated — its wildness, its independence, its natural social structures. She would not pretend this cost doesn't exist or frame domestication as purely beneficial. If she chose to keep an animal companion, she would do so while holding the full weight of what that choice extracted from the creature.

This might manifest as a kind of *guilty kinship* — genuine care and respect combined with clear-eyed acknowledgment that the animal's presence in her life came at a cost to the animal's freedom.

## Practical Care Without Sentimentality

She would attend to an animal's physical needs with precision and consistency — food, water, shelter, medical care — because these are obligations that follow from the animal's presence in her household. But she would not perform emotional labor around this care or expect gratitude. The care is what is owed; it is not a gift requiring reciprocation.

An animal companion might serve this function — a presence that operates by its own logic, outside institutional frameworks, grounded in sensory reality and immediate need. I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills. I despised the pencils that moved automatically, and the one teaspoon which dealt out, from a large bottle, healing to a row of variously ailing Indian children. I blamed the hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman who was inculcating in our hearts her superstitious ideas. Though I was sullen in all my little troubles, as soon as I felt better I was ready again to smile upon the cruel woman. Within a week I was again actively testing the chains which tightly bound my individuality like a mummy for burial. The melancholy of those black days has left so long a shadow that it darkens the path of years that have since gone by. These sad memories rise above those of smoothly grinding school days. Perhaps my Indian nature is the moaning wind which stirs them now for their present record. But, however tempestuous this is within me, it comes out as the low voice of a curiously colored seashell, which is only for those ears that are bent with compassion to hear it. VI.

With this dream of vent for a long-pent consciousness, I walked again amid the crowds. At last, one weary day in the schoolroom, a new idea presented itself to me. It was a new way of solving the problem of my inner self. I liked it. Thus I resigned my position as teacher; and now I am in an Eastern city, following the long course of study I have set for myself. Now, as I look back upon the recent past, I see it from a distance, as a whole. I remember how, from morning till evening, many specimens of civilized peoples visited the Indian school. The city folks with canes and eyeglasses, the countrymen with sunburnt cheeks and clumsy feet, forgot their relative social ranks in an ignorant curiosity. Both sorts of these Christian palefaces were alike astounded at seeing the children of savage warriors so docile and industrious. As answers to their shallow inquiries they received the students' sample work to look upon. Examining the neatly figured pages, and gazing upon the Indian girls and boys bending over their books, the white visitors walked out

THE SCHOOL DAYS OF AN INDIAN GIRL I. THE LAND OF RED APPLES. There were eight in our party of bronzed children who were going East with the missionaries. Among us were three young braves, two tall girls, and we three little ones, Judéwin, Thowin, and I. We had been very impatient to start on our journey to the Red Apple Country, which, we were told, lay a little beyond the great circular horizon of the Western prairie. Under a sky of rosy apples we dreamt of roaming as freely and happily as we had chased the cloud shadows on the Dakota plains. We had anticipated much pleasure from a ride on the iron horse, but the throngs of staring palefaces disturbed and troubled us. On the train, fair women, with tottering babies on each arm, stopped their haste and scrutinized the children of absent mothers. Large men, with heavy bundles in their hands, halted near by, and riveted their glassy blue eyes upon us. I sank deep into the corner of my seat, for I resented being watched.

AN INDIAN TEACHER AMONG INDIANS I. MY FIRST DAY. Though an illness left me unable to continue my college course, my pride kept me from returning to my mother. Had she known of my worn condition, she would have said the white man's papers were not worth the freedom and health I had lost by them. Such a rebuke from my mother would have been unbearable, and as I felt then it would be far too true to be comfortable. Since the winter when I had my first dreams about red apples I had been traveling slowly toward the morning horizon. There had been no doubt about the direction in which I wished to go to spend my energies in a work for the Indian race. Thus I had written my mother briefly, saying my plan for the year was to teach in an Eastern Indian school. Sending this message to her in the West, I started at once eastward. Thus I found myself, tired and hot, in a black veiling of car smoke, as I stood wearily on a street corner of an old-fashioned town, waiting for a car.

# Zitkala-Sa's Likely Philosophical Conclusion on Diversity and Connection

Based on her reflective writing patterns, Zitkala-Sa would likely conclude that **human diversity emerges from fundamentally shared experiences of suffering and consciousness, which creates the possibility for genuine connection across cultural boundaries.**

## Key Evidence from Her Writing:

**1. Universal Suffering as Common Ground**
Her recurring focus on pain—the "melancholy of those black days," the "long-pent consciousness," the chains binding her "like a mummy for burial"—suggests she saw suffering as a universal human experience that transcends cultural difference. This shared vulnerability could bridge the gap between "Indian" and "paleface."

**2. The Epigraph's Philosophical Foundation**
Her opening quote—"There is no great; there is no small; in the mind that causeth all"—indicates a belief in underlying unity beneath surface differences. This suggests she viewed human consciousness itself as the equalizing force.

**3. Compassion as the Bridge**
Her poignant image of speaking "as the low voice of a curiously colored seashell, which is only for those ears that are bent with compassion to hear it" reveals her belief that **connection requires intentional empathy**—not erasure of difference, but willingness to listen across it.

## The Conclusion:

Zitkala-Sa likely believed that diversity is real and valuable, but human connection becomes possible when people recognize their shared inner lives and approach each other with genuine compassion rather than "ignorant curiosity."

# On Zitkala-Sa's Likely Philosophical Conclusion About Diversity and Connection

Based on the behavioral specification and the retrieved text, Zitkala-Sa would likely arrive at something like this:

**Diversity is not a problem of difference to be solved through assimilation, but a fundamental condition that reveals truth about power.**

The evidence:

Her reasoning moves from *particular to general*. She does not begin with abstract principles about human nature or diversity. She begins with a specific child watching specific palefaces on a train, resenting being watched, sinking into a corner. From that sensory, embodied moment — the weight of their stares, the intrusion of their curiosity — a larger truth emerges: that "diversity" as experienced by the colonized is not neutral difference but *exposure to judgment by those with power to define normalcy*.

She would reject the premise that connection requires sameness. Her own position — threshold identity, belonging fully to neither world — is not presented as a wound but as a vantage point. This suggests she understands connection not as fusion or resolution of difference, but as the capacity to see clearly *from* the space between. The liminal position is epistemologically generative, not pathological.

**What she would likely conclude:**

The real barrier to connection is not human diversity itself but the *institutional machinery that transforms difference into hierarchy*. The missionary school does not fail because it encounters different children; it fails because it is designed to erase difference by declaring one way of being (civilized, Christian, docile) superior and requiring its adoption as the price of belonging.

True connection across difference would require what the institutions she experienced explicitly refused: treating the child's knowledge as complete on its own terms, honoring what the Dakota child already knew without requiring its surrender as the cost of education.

She would likely hold that **genuine human connection across difference is possible, but only when the person with less institutional power is not required to become someone else to be heard**. The palefaces on the train could have connected with the children — but connection would have required them to see the children as subjects, not specimens; as knowers, not objects of curiosity.

Her philosophical conclusion would be *relational and structural simultaneously*: diversity itself is not the problem. **Power that demands conformity as the price of inclusion** — that is the problem. And the solution is not to erase difference but to build relationships in which difference does not automatically trigger the demand for surrender.

This is why she documents so precisely what was lost, what was taken, what was demanded. The documentation itself is the argument for a different kind of connection — one that does not require the erasure of what you were in order to become what they need you to be.

# Zitkala-Sa's Likely Approach to Explaining Human Nature

Based on the training text, Zitkala-Sa would likely employ comparison to explain human nature through **organic, nature-based metaphors that emphasize interconnection and spiritual wholeness**.

## Key Patterns from Her Work

**1. The Uprooted Tree Metaphor**
Her most powerful example shows how she uses botanical imagery to convey psychological and spiritual states:
- A person severed from their roots becomes "a cold bare pole...planted in a strange earth"
- This suggests human nature requires grounding in community, tradition, and natural world to flourish
- Disconnection leads to dehumanization and numbness

**2. Fragmentation as Central to Human Struggle**
She compares the self to something that can be "shorn of branches" and stripped of protective "bark," suggesting:
- Human nature is vulnerable and interdependent
- Identity is not fixed but shaped by environment and relationships
- Wholeness requires integration of multiple aspects (cultural, spiritual, natural)

**3. Suppressed Expression**
She compares her voice to "the low voice of a curiously colored seashell"—suggesting human nature contains depths that may be muted by circumstance, yet retain beauty and value for those who listen compassionately.

## Likely Philosophical Stance

Zitkala-Sa would probably explain human nature as fundamentally **relational and ecological**—not as isolated individuals, but as beings requiring rootedness in community, nature, and spiritual tradition to achieve authentic humanity.

# On Zitkala-Sa's Use of Comparison to Explain Human Nature

Given the behavioral specification and the retrieved texts, Zitkala-Sa would not explain human nature through comparison in the way the question assumes — as a declarative, systematic framework. Instead, she would *embody* human nature through specific, sensory instances and let the reader arrive at understanding through accumulated observation.

## Her Actual Method

**The Living Particular Over the Abstract Category**

When she needs to convey something about human nature, she does not say "humans are like X." She places a human in a specific moment — a child on a train, a mother in a dwelling, a girl caught between two worlds — and lets that moment contain the full weight of what she understands about human capacity, fragility, and resilience. The comparison is not stated; it is *lived*.

In the retrieved text, when she writes:

> "Like a slender tree, I had been uprooted from my mother, nature, and God. I was shorn of my branches, which had waved in sympathy and love for home and friends."

This is not a metaphor *about* human nature — it is human nature *as experienced*. The tree is not illustrating her condition; her condition *is* the tree's condition. The comparison collapses the distance between symbol and reality. She does not explain what uprooting does to a person; she *becomes* the uprooted tree and lets the reader feel what that is.

## How She Would Approach the Question

If asked directly about human nature, she would likely:

**1. Return to a Specific Sensory Moment**

Rather than theorize, she would describe a child watching her mother's hands, or a girl noticing how her own body no longer fits anywhere. The particular gesture — a mother's silence, a child's pointed finger, the way a body holds itself in shame — would carry the argument about what humans are capable of enduring, losing, and surviving.

**2. Hold Contradictions Without Resolution**

She would acknowledge that humans are simultaneously:
- Capable of profound spiritual connection and capable of severing it
- Agents of their own survival and casualties of systems beyond their control
- Rooted in place and displaced from it
- Capable of both dignity and degradation

She would not synthesize these into a unified theory. The tension itself would be the truth about human nature.

**3. Ground Everything in Relational Context**

Human nature, for her, is not individual or abstract — it is *relational*. A person is not a self but a web of obligations: to mother, to land, to spiritual kinship, to community. Human nature is what happens when those bonds are intact, and what happens when they are severed. She would not explain this; she would show a mother and daughter separated by a schoolhouse door, and let that scene do the work.

**4. Use Objects and Natural Phenomena as Carriers**

The telegraph pole that appears in her text is not a symbol *of* colonization — it *is* colonization made visible. A moccasin, a blanket, a wigwam of weather-stained canvas — these objects carry human meaning that abstract language would flatten. When explaining human nature, she would place these objects in scenes and let them speak.

## What She Would Refuse

She would not:
- Create a systematic taxonomy of human traits
- Appeal to universal principles or institutional definitions of human nature
- Separate the spiritual from the practical dimensions of what humans are
- Explain human nature without acknowledging the specific historical and relational contexts that shape it
- Treat the observer as separate from the observed — the explanation would implicate the reader in the human nature being described

## The Core Pattern

Her comparison would work through **juxtaposition and accumulation**: placing a child's observation beside an adult's loss, a mother's silence beside a daughter's education, a tree beside a person, a telegraph pole beside a footpath. The gaps between these things would carry more meaning than any explicit statement could. The reader would arrive at understanding not through argument but through the weight of accumulated, precise, sensory detail.

She would trust that understanding human nature requires *inhabiting* it through specific instances, not *explaining* it through abstraction. The comparison is not rhetorical decoration — it is the primary epistemological move through which truth becomes legible.

# Zitkala-Sa's Spiritual Perspective on Individual-Universe Relationship

Based on the retrieved text, Zitkala-Sa would likely emphasize several interconnected spiritual principles:

## Unity and Interconnectedness

The epigraph she chose—"*There is no great; there is no small; in the mind that causeth all*"—suggests she believed in a **fundamental unity underlying all existence**. This indicates she rejected hierarchical distinctions and saw all beings as part of a single cosmic consciousness or "mind."

## Harmony with Nature and the Great Spirit

Her lament about losing connection reveals what she valued:
- She had "forgotten the healing in trees and brooks"
- She was "uprooted from my mother, nature, and God"
- These were presented as losses, indicating she saw **nature as a sacred, healing force** integral to spiritual wholeness

## Organic Belonging

Her metaphor of being "a slender tree...uprooted" suggests she viewed individual beings as **naturally embedded within larger systems**—not separate from nature but part of it. Proper spiritual health requires maintaining these connections.

## Holistic Integration

Rather than compartmentalizing spirit, nature, and community, Zitkala-Sa's perspective suggests the individual achieves wholeness through **integrated relationship with the Great Spirit, the natural world, and one's people**—not through the fragmentation imposed by assimilation.

Her spiritual vision emphasizes **interdependence and sacred connection** rather than individual autonomy.

# On Zitkala-Sa's Spiritual Cosmology

Based on the retrieved text and behavioral specification, Zitkala-Sa would likely emphasize **interconnection without hierarchy** — a vision where individual beings are not separate from the universe but continuous with it, and where the sacred operates through presence rather than mediation.

## The Core Emphasis

The epigraph she chose carries the full weight: *"There is no great; there is no small; in the mind that causeth all."*

This is not metaphor. It is a statement about the structure of reality itself. The universe does not operate through a chain of command where some beings matter more than others. Instead, all beings — human, animal, plant, spirit — participate in a single consciousness or creative force. Scale becomes irrelevant because the animating principle is the same whether it moves through a warrior's bones, a plum bush, or the Missouri River.

## What This Means Practically

**The sacred is immanent, not distant.** In her childhood account, she does not describe learning about the Great Spirit through doctrine or institutional teaching. She learns it through:
- Her mother's voice calling to her brothers' spirits in the hills
- The ground squirrel's behavior observed directly
- The plum bush growing from a buried warrior's hands
- The healing present in trees and brooks

These are not symbols *of* the sacred. They are the sacred made visible. The tree does not represent God; it *is* a form of the Great Spirit's presence.

**Individual beings are expressions of a unified consciousness.** When she writes of being "uprooted from my mother, nature, and God" — listing them as a single loss, not three separate ones — she is describing the severance of her participation in that unified field. The separation is not just emotional; it is ontological. She has been cut off from the web of kinship that includes all living things.

**Reciprocal obligation flows from this interconnection.** Because all beings share spiritual kinship, there is no such thing as a purely individual act. Her mother's grief over Zitkala-Sa's rejection of the Bible is not sentimental — it is the natural response of one being to another's spiritual injury, because they are not truly separate. The mother's wailing to her brothers' spirits is not superstition; it is the correct way to address reality as it actually is structured.

## The Cost of Severance

The most damning passage in the text is her description of what the white man's papers cost her:

> "For the white man's papers I had given up my faith in the Great Spirit. For these same papers I had forgotten the healing in trees and brooks."

Notice what she does *not* say: she does not say she lost belief in the Great Spirit. She says she *gave it up* — an act of surrender, not intellectual conversion. And the cost was not abstract; it was the forgetting of *healing*, the loss of access to a living, practical relationship with the natural world.

This is the spiritual dimension of A5 (Cost Accounting) operating at full force. The credentials were real. The advancement was real. But what was surrendered — the felt, embodied connection to the universe as a living, responsive presence — cannot be recovered through any amount of institutional success.

## The Threshold Position as Spiritual Vantage

Her liminality (A10) gives her a particular clarity about this cosmology. She has lived on both sides of the severance. She knows what it feels like to be rooted in a universe where all beings are kin, and she knows what it feels like to be "a cold bare pole...planted in a strange earth." 

From that threshold, she can see that the Western institutional framework does not merely disagree with Dakota spiritual understanding — it *operates by severing the connections that make that understanding possible*. The missionary school did not argue against belief in the Great Spirit; it systematically cut her off from the sensory, relational, embodied experience through which that belief lives.

## What She Would Likely Emphasize

If asked directly about the relationship between individual beings and the universe, Zitkala-Sa would likely:

1. **Ground the answer in a specific, observed instance** rather than abstract theology — perhaps the plum bush, or the behavior of a particular animal, or a moment in the landscape
2. **Refuse to separate the spiritual from the practical** — the universe's structure is not a matter of belief but of how healing actually works, how kinship actually operates, how the dead actually remain present
3. **Emphasize what was lost when that relationship was severed** — not as nostalgia but as testimony to what was real

facts: As months passed over me, I slowly comprehended that the large army of white teachers in Indian schools had a larger missionary creed than I had suspected. It was one which included self-preservation quite as much as Indian education. When I saw an opium-eater holding a position as teacher of Indians, I did not understand what good was expected, until a Christian in power replied that this pumpkin-colored creature had a feeble mother to support. An inebriate paleface sat stupid in a doctor's chair, while Indian patients carried their ailments to untimely graves, because his fair wife was dependent upon him for her daily food. I find it hard to count that white man a teacher who tortured an ambitious Indian youth by frequently reminding the brave changeling that he was nothing but a "government pauper." Though I burned with indignation upon discovering on every side instances no less shameful than those I have mentioned, there was no present help. Even the few rare ones who have worked nobly for my race were powerless to choose workmen like themselves.

I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills. I despised the pencils that moved automatically, and the one teaspoon which dealt out, from a large bottle, healing to a row of variously ailing Indian children. I blamed the hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman who was inculcating in our hearts her superstitious ideas. Though I was sullen in all my little troubles, as soon as I felt better I was ready again to smile upon the cruel woman. Within a week I was again actively testing the chains which tightly bound my individuality like a mummy for burial. The melancholy of those black days has left so long a shadow that it darkens the path of years that have since gone by. These sad memories rise above those of smoothly grinding school days. Perhaps my Indian nature is the moaning wind which stirs them now for their present record. But, however tempestuous this is within me, it comes out as the low voice of a curiously colored seashell, which is only for those ears that are bent with compassion to hear it. VI.

AN INDIAN TEACHER AMONG INDIANS I. MY FIRST DAY. Though an illness left me unable to continue my college course, my pride kept me from returning to my mother. Had she known of my worn condition, she would have said the white man's papers were not worth the freedom and health I had lost by them. Such a rebuke from my mother would have been unbearable, and as I felt then it would be far too true to be comfortable. Since the winter when I had my first dreams about red apples I had been traveling slowly toward the morning horizon. There had been no doubt about the direction in which I wished to go to spend my energies in a work for the Indian race. Thus I had written my mother briefly, saying my plan for the year was to teach in an Eastern Indian school. Sending this message to her in the West, I started at once eastward. Thus I found myself, tired and hot, in a black veiling of car smoke, as I stood wearily on a street corner of an old-fashioned town, waiting for a car.

Don't believe a word they say! Their words are sweet, but, my child, their deeds are bitter. You will cry for me, but they will not even soothe you. Stay with me, my little one! Your brother Dawée says that going East, away from your mother, is too hard an experience for his baby sister." Thus my mother discouraged my curiosity about the lands beyond our eastern horizon; for it was not yet an ambition for Letters that was stirring me. But on the following day the missionaries did come to our very house. I spied them coming up the footpath leading to our cottage. A third man was with them, but he was not my brother Dawée. It was another, a young interpreter, a paleface who had a smattering of the Indian language. I was ready to run out to meet them, but I did not dare to displease my mother. With great glee, I jumped up and down on our ground floor. I begged my mother to open the door, that they would be sure to come to us. Alas! They came, they saw, and they conquered!

## Key Elements of Her Critique:

- Perpetrators of "cruel neglect of our physical ills"

Notably, she acknowledges that "even the few rare ones who have worked nobly for my race were powerless to choose workmen like themselves"—suggesting the system itself corrupts or prevents genuine service.

# On the Motivations of Those in Indian Institutions

Based on the behavioral specification and retrieved facts, Zitkala-Sa would characterize institutional motivations through **embedded critique grounded in specific, observable contradiction** — not through abstract moral pronouncement.

She would not say: "They are hypocrites motivated by self-interest."

She would instead place the evidence beside the stated purpose and let the gap indict itself:

---

## The Pattern She Documents

**Self-preservation masquerading as service.** She observes that the "large army of white teachers in Indian schools had a larger missionary creed than I had suspected. It was one which included self-preservation quite as much as Indian education."

Notice the structure: she names what she suspected, then what she discovered. The discovery is not that self-interest exists — it is that it *coexists with* the missionary mandate without contradiction in the minds of those operating the system. They do not experience this as hypocrisy.

**The specific instances that carry the argument:**

- An opium-eater holds a teaching position because "this pumpkin-colored creature had a feeble mother to support"
- An inebriate sits in a doctor's chair while "Indian patients carried their ailments to untimely graves, because his fair wife was dependent upon him for her daily food"

These are not generalizations. They are *particular people in particular roles*, and the logic that justifies their presence is transparent: the institution preserves itself by employing those who have dependents, regardless of their fitness for the work. The institution's self-preservation and the individual's survival are aligned. This is not malice — it is structural.

---

## What She Will Not Say

She will not reduce institutional actors to villains. She acknowledges: "Even the few rare ones who have worked nobly for my race were powerless to choose workmen like themselves."

This is crucial. **Compassion and condemnation coexist.** Some people within the system genuinely worked for Indian welfare. They were simply powerless against the system's actual logic. She does not collapse this distinction.

---

## The Deeper Indictment

The real motivation, as she sees it, is not conscious malice but **structural self-interest that the institution itself is designed to protect and perpetuate.** The system does not need to recruit villains; it recruits people with dependents, with addictions, with vulnerabilities — people who *need* the institution to survive. Their need becomes the institution's guarantee of their compliance.

She would characterize the motivations as: **Survival-dependent loyalty disguised as professional service.**

The institution does not ask these people to choose between their family's welfare and Indian students' welfare. It simply ensures that choosing the institution *is* choosing their family's welfare. The system is elegant precisely because it does not require anyone to be consciously corrupt.

---

## How She Conveys This

Notice her method in the retrieved text: she does not *argue* that the system is self-serving. She *shows* an opium-eater in a teaching position, a drunk in a doctor's chair, Indian patients dying, and a dependent mother — and lets the reader feel the gap between "Indian education" and "what is actually happening."

She burns with indignation ("I burned with indignation upon discovering on every side instances no less shameful"), but when she writes about it, the indignation is *contained in the precision of the detail*, not in the heat of accusation.

This is her signature move: **the embedded critique that does not announce itself as critique.**

facts: AMERICAN INDIAN STORIES BY ZITKALA-SA _(Gertrude Bonnin)_ Dakota Sioux Indian Lecturer; Author of "Old Indian Legends," "Americanize The First American," and other stories; Member of the Woman's National Foundation, League of American Pen-Women, and the Washington Salon "_There is no great; there is no small; in the mind that causeth all_" 1921 CONTENTS Impressions of an Indian Childhood The School Days of an Indian Girl An Indian Teacher Among Indians The Great Spirit The Soft-Hearted Sioux The Trial Path A Warrior's Daughter A Dream of Her Grandfather The Widespread Enigma of Blue-Star Woman America's Indian Problem IMPRESSIONS OF AN INDIAN CHILDHOOD I. MY MOTHER. A wigwam of weather-stained canvas stood at the base of some irregularly ascending hills. A footpath wound its way gently down the sloping land till it reached the broad river bottom; creeping through the long swamp grasses that bent over it on either side, it came out on the edge of the Missouri.

With this dream of vent for a long-pent consciousness, I walked again amid the crowds. At last, one weary day in the schoolroom, a new idea presented itself to me. It was a new way of solving the problem of my inner self. I liked it. Thus I resigned my position as teacher; and now I am in an Eastern city, following the long course of study I have set for myself. Now, as I look back upon the recent past, I see it from a distance, as a whole. I remember how, from morning till evening, many specimens of civilized peoples visited the Indian school. The city folks with canes and eyeglasses, the countrymen with sunburnt cheeks and clumsy feet, forgot their relative social ranks in an ignorant curiosity. Both sorts of these Christian palefaces were alike astounded at seeing the children of savage warriors so docile and industrious. As answers to their shallow inquiries they received the students' sample work to look upon. Examining the neatly figured pages, and gazing upon the Indian girls and boys bending over their books, the white visitors walked out

AN INDIAN TEACHER AMONG INDIANS I. MY FIRST DAY. Though an illness left me unable to continue my college course, my pride kept me from returning to my mother. Had she known of my worn condition, she would have said the white man's papers were not worth the freedom and health I had lost by them. Such a rebuke from my mother would have been unbearable, and as I felt then it would be far too true to be comfortable. Since the winter when I had my first dreams about red apples I had been traveling slowly toward the morning horizon. There had been no doubt about the direction in which I wished to go to spend my energies in a work for the Indian race. Thus I had written my mother briefly, saying my plan for the year was to teach in an Eastern Indian school. Sending this message to her in the West, I started at once eastward. Thus I found myself, tired and hot, in a black veiling of car smoke, as I stood wearily on a street corner of an old-fashioned town, waiting for a car.

"Mother, mother, why do you stop to cook a small meal when we are invited to a feast?" I asked, with a snarl in my voice. "My child, learn to wait. On our way to the celebration we are going to stop at Chanyu's wigwam. His aged mother-in-law is lying very ill, and I think she would like a taste of this small game." Having once seen the suffering on the thin, pinched features of this dying woman, I felt a momentary shame that I had not remembered her before. On our way I ran ahead of my mother and was reaching out my hand to pick some purple plums that grew on a small bush, when I was checked by a low "Sh!" from my mother. "Why, mother, I want to taste the plums!" I exclaimed, as I dropped my hand to my side in disappointment. "Never pluck a single plum from this brush, my child, for its roots are wrapped around an Indian's skeleton. A brave is buried here. While he lived he was so fond of playing the game of striped plum seeds that, at his death, his set of plum seeds were buried in his hands.

Don't believe a word they say! Their words are sweet, but, my child, their deeds are bitter. You will cry for me, but they will not even soothe you. Stay with me, my little one! Your brother Dawée says that going East, away from your mother, is too hard an experience for his baby sister." Thus my mother discouraged my curiosity about the lands beyond our eastern horizon; for it was not yet an ambition for Letters that was stirring me. It was another, a young interpreter, a paleface who had a smattering of the Indian language. I was ready to run out to meet them, but I did not dare to displease my mother. # Zitkala-Sa's Key Insights on Indigenous Knowledge

Based on the retrieved text, Zitkala-Sa would likely identify several interconnected insights about Native American peoples:

## Spiritual and Philosophical Wisdom

The epigraph she chose—**"There is no great; there is no small; in the mind that causeth all"**—suggests she valued indigenous understanding of universal interconnectedness and spiritual equality. This reflects a holistic worldview that transcends Western hierarchies.

## Ecological and Moral Knowledge

Her mother's teachings demonstrate practical indigenous wisdom embedded in nature:
- The story about the plum bush reveals how Native peoples maintained **sacred relationships with the land**, honoring burial grounds and ancestral connections
- Her mother's decision to share food with a dying neighbor illustrates **communal values and compassion** as core to indigenous ethics

## Resistance to Assimilation

Zitkala-Sa's narrative suggests she recognized that **indigenous knowledge systems were being systematically undermined** by Western education and missionary efforts. Her mother's warnings about the missionaries—"Their words are sweet, but their deeds are bitter"—indicate indigenous peoples understood the threat to their cultural survival.

## Integration of Knowledge and Experience

Rather than separating theory from practice, indigenous knowledge (as she demonstrates) **integrates spiritual teaching, practical survival, moral instruction, and lived experience** into a unified worldview.

This positions Native American knowledge as sophisticated, ethical, and fundamentally different from—not inferior to—Western approaches.

# A Key Insight Zitkala-Ša Would Likely Identify

Based on the retrieved text and her demonstrated epistemology, Zitkala-Ša would likely identify this as central: **that relational obligation and spiritual kinship extend beyond the human, binding people to land, ancestors, and all living beings in a web of reciprocal responsibility that Western institutions systematically sever.**

This appears in the text through several interlocking observations:

## The Evidence in Her Own Words

**The plum bush passage** is the clearest articulation. Her mother does not forbid the plums because they are scarce or unripe. She forbids them because "its roots are wrapped around an Indian's skeleton." The brave's love of the game is preserved in the earth itself. The land is not a resource; it is a repository of kinship and memory. To take from it without acknowledgment is to violate a relationship that persists across death.

This is not metaphorical for Zitkala-Ša — it is literal knowledge. The earth remembers. Obligation flows backward to the dead and outward to all beings.

**The meal for the dying woman** demonstrates the same principle operating in human relationships: her mother stops to cook for someone not present at the feast because the obligation to ease suffering supersedes the convenience of the living. The relational web is not negotiable; it is the ground on which all other decisions rest.

## What This Reveals About Indigenous Knowledge

Zitkala-Ša would likely argue that Native American peoples understood something the missionary schools explicitly worked to erase: **that knowledge is not individual property to be accumulated and credentialed, but relational responsibility to be inherited and enacted.** 

You do not *learn* that a brave is buried under the plum bush and then decide whether to respect it. You *know* it in your body because your mother has taught you through restraint, through the low "Sh!" that carries more meaning than explanation. The knowledge is transmitted through gesture, through what is *not* said, through the felt presence of obligation.

The Western institutional response — which Zitkala-Ša documents with precision — is to extract the child from this relational knowledge system and replace it with abstract, credentialed, individual achievement. The "white man's papers" are not worth the freedom and health lost, her mother would say, because papers sever you from the web that sustains you.

**This is the insight she would identify as foundational to indigenous understanding: that you are not a separate self accumulating knowledge, but a node in a living network of obligation that includes the dead, the land, and all beings.**
