facts: All the morning I looked about me, recognizing old familiar sky lines of rugged bluffs and round-topped hills. By the roadside I caught glimpses of various plants whose sweet roots were delicacies among my people. When I saw the first cone-shaped wigwam, I could not help uttering an exclamation which caused my driver a sudden jump out of his drowsy nodding. At noon, as we drove through the eastern edge of the reservation, I grew very impatient and restless. Constantly I wondered what my mother would say upon seeing her little daughter grown tall. I had not written her the day of my arrival, thinking I would surprise her. Crossing a ravine thicketed with low shrubs and plum bushes, we approached a large yellow acre of wild sunflowers. Just beyond this nature's garden we drew near to my mother's cottage. Close by the log cabin stood a little canvas-covered wigwam. The driver stopped in front of the open door, and in a long moment my mother appeared at the threshold.

But I know my daughter must suffer keenly in this experiment. For her sake, I dread to tell you my reply to the missionaries. Go, tell them that they may take my little daughter, and that the Great Spirit shall not fail to reward them according to their hearts." Wrapped in my heavy blanket, I walked with my mother to the carriage that was soon to take us to the iron horse. I was happy. I met my playmates, who were also wearing their best thick blankets. We showed one another our new beaded moccasins, and the width of the belts that girdled our new dresses. Soon we were being drawn rapidly away by the white man's horses. When I saw the lonely figure of my mother vanish in the distance, a sense of regret settled heavily upon me. I felt suddenly weak, as if I might fall limp to the ground. I was in the hands of strangers whom my mother did not fully trust. I no longer felt free to be myself, or to voice my own feelings. The tears trickled down my cheeks, and I buried my face in the folds of my blanket.

This deplorable situation was the effect of my brief course in the East, and the unsatisfactory "teenth" in a girl's years. It was under these trying conditions that, one bright afternoon, as I sat restless and unhappy in my mother's cabin, I caught the sound of the spirited step of my brother's pony on the road which passed by our dwelling. Soon I heard the wheels of a light buckboard, and Dawée's familiar "Ho!" to his pony. He alighted upon the bare ground in front of our house. Tying his pony to one of the projecting corner logs of the low-roofed cottage, he stepped upon the wooden doorstep. I met him there with a hurried greeting, and, as I passed by, he looked a quiet "What?" into my eyes. When he began talking with my mother, I slipped the rope from the pony's bridle. Seizing the reins and bracing my feet against the dashboard, I wheeled around in an instant. The pony was ever ready to try his speed. Looking backward, I saw Dawée waving his hand to me. I turned with the curve in the road and disappeared.

I was always slight, and my serious illness in the early spring had made me look rather frail and languid. His quick eye measured my height and breadth. Then he looked into my face. I imagined that a visible shadow flitted across his countenance as he let my hand fall. I knew he was no other than my employer. "Ah ha! so you are the little Indian girl who created the excitement among the college orators!" he said, more to himself than to me. I thought I heard a subtle note of disappointment in his voice. Looking in from where he stood, with one sweeping glance, he asked if I lacked anything for my room. After he turned to go, I listened to his step until it grew faint and was lost in the distance. I was aware that my car-smoked appearance had not concealed the lines of pain on my face. For a short moment my spirit laughed at my ill fortune, and I entertained the idea of exerting myself to make an improvement. But as I tossed my hat off a leaden weakness came over me, and I felt as if years of weariness lay like water-soaked logs upon me.

Mother sat in a chair, and I was hanging on her knee. Within the last two seasons my big brother Dawée had returned from a three years' education in the East, and his coming back influenced my mother to take a farther step from her native way of living. First it was a change from the buffalo skin to the white man's canvas that covered our wigwam. Now she had given up her wigwam

My mother used to say to me, as I was almost bounding away for the old people: "Wait a moment before you invite any one. If other plans are being discussed, do not interfere, but go elsewhere." The old folks knew the meaning of my pauses; and often they coaxed my confidence by asking, "What do you seek, little granddaughter?" "My mother says you are to come to our tepee this evening," I instantly exploded, and breathed the freer afterwards. "Yes, yes, gladly, gladly I shall come!" each replied. Rising at once and carrying their blankets across one shoulder, they flocked leisurely from their various wigwams toward our dwelling. My mission done, I ran back, skipping and jumping with delight. All out of breath, I told my mother almost the exact words of the answers to my invitation. Frequently she asked, "What were they doing when you entered their tepee?" This taught me to remember all I saw at a single glance. Often I told my mother my impressions without being questioned.

# Response to Return Home

Based on the behavioral specification and retrieved facts, Zitkala-Sa's emotional response upon returning home would be **layered and contradictory—not a simple homecoming but a collision of belonging and estrangement**.

## The Immediate Response: Recognition and Disorientation

The retrieved text shows her arriving at her mother's cottage after years away:

> "All the morning I looked about me, recognizing old familiar sky lines of rugged bluffs and round-topped hills... Constantly I wondered what my mother would say upon seeing her little daughter grown tall."

This is anticipation mixed with uncertainty. She *recognizes* the landscape—the bluffs, the plants whose roots were "delicacies among my people"—but she is also returning as someone transformed by the very institution that separated her from this place. The recognition is embodied (A2: embodied knowing), but it arrives in a body that is no longer entirely hers.

## The Deeper Current: Grief Without Performance

What matters more than her stated emotions is what the text *shows* through sensory detail (M1: reasoning signature through accumulation):

- The "long moment" before her mother appears at the threshold
- The "sense of regret" that "settled heavily" upon her when she saw her mother vanish in the distance
- The physical collapse: "I felt suddenly weak, as if I might fall limp to the ground"
- The tears buried in her blanket

This is not performed grief. It is grief carried in the body—in the weakness, the heaviness, the tears hidden rather than displayed. She does not declare her sorrow; she inhabits it.

## The Structural Reality: She Cannot Fully Return

The critical dimension (A10: threshold identity) is that **she is no longer entirely of this world, nor of the world she came from**. The text reveals this through what she *cannot do*:

> "I was in the hands of strangers whom my mother did not fully trust. I no longer felt free to be myself, or to voice my own feelings."

Even in her mother's presence, she has lost the freedom to be herself. This is not a temporary adjustment—it is the permanent condition of someone who has been separated and educated into a different framework. Her mother's world is still legible to her (she recognizes the landscape, understands the protocols), but she no longer *inhabits* it naturally.

## What She Would Not Say Aloud

Given her communication pattern (M1: suppressed direct confrontation, embedded critique), she would likely:

- **Not** articulate the full weight of what was lost
- **Not** blame her mother for allowing the separation
- **Not** perform gratitude for her education or its "opportunities"
- **Instead** move through the familiar routines with precision, observing details, carrying the weight of the gap between what was promised and what was lived

The cost accounting (A5) would be silent but total: she has gained education and credentials at the price of belonging nowhere completely.

## The Kinship Anchor

What would ground her emotionally (A1: kinship ground) is not the landscape or the institutions, but **her mother's presence itself**—the relational bond that survived the separation. But even this is complicated: her mother made the choice to let her go, believing the missionaries' promises. That trust was violated, and Zitkala-Sa would carry this knowledge without ever fully articulating it to her mother.

---

**In sum:** Her emotional response would be **homecoming as estrangement**—recognition without return, love without ease, presence without full belonging. The emotion would be carried in her body, in the precision of what she observes, in what she does not say. She would be home and not home simultaneously, and this tension would not resolve.

It was next to impossible to leave the iron routine after the civilizing machine had once begun its day's buzzing; and as it was inbred in me to suffer in silence rather than to appeal to the ears of one whose open eyes could not see my pain, I have many times trudged in the day's harness heavy-footed, like a dumb sick brute. At her deathbed I stood weeping, as the paleface woman sat near her moistening the dry lips. Among the folds of the bedclothes I saw the open pages of the white man's Bible. The dying Indian girl talked disconnectedly of Jesus the Christ and the paleface who was cooling her swollen hands and feet. I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills.

The dim yellow light of the braided muslin burning in a small vessel of oil flickered and sizzled in the awful silent storm which followed my rejection of the Bible. Now my wrath against the fates consumed my tears before they reached my eyes. I sat stony, with a bowed head. My mother threw a shawl over her head and shoulders, and stepped out into the night. After an uncertain solitude, I was suddenly aroused by a loud cry piercing the night. It was my mother's voice wailing among the barren hills which held the bones of buried warriors. She called aloud for her brothers' spirits to support her in her helpless misery. My fingers Grey icy cold, as I realized that my unrestrained tears had betrayed my suffering to her, and she was grieving for me. Before she returned, though I knew she was on her way, for she had ceased her weeping, I extinguished the light, and leaned my head on the window sill. Many schemes of running away from my surroundings hovered about in my mind. A few more moons of such a turmoil drove me away to the eastern school.

A brave is buried here. While he lived he was so fond of playing the game of striped plum seeds that, at his death, his set of plum seeds were buried in his hands. After that time I halted in my ramblings whenever I came in sight of the plum bush. I grew sober with awe, and was alert to hear a long-drawn-out whistle rise from the roots of it. Though I had never heard with my own ears this strange whistle of departed spirits, yet I had listened so frequently to hear the old folks describe it that I knew I should recognize it at once. The lasting impression of that day, as I recall it now, is what my mother told me about the dead man's plum bush. VI. THE GROUND SQUIRREL. In the busy autumn days my cousin Warca-Ziwin's mother came to our wigwam to help my mother preserve foods for our winter use. I was very fond of my aunt, because she was not so quiet as my mother.

"Mother, I am going East! I like big red apples, and I want to ride on the iron horse! Mother, say yes!" I pleaded. My mother said nothing. The missionaries waited in silence; and my eyes began to blur with tears, though I struggled to choke them back. The corners of my mouth twitched, and my mother saw me. "I am not ready to give you any word," she said to them. "Tomorrow I shall send you my answer by my son." With this they left us. Alone with my mother, I yielded to my tears, and cried aloud, shaking my head so as not to hear what she was saying to me. This was the first time I had ever been so unwilling to give up my own desire that I refused to hearken to my mother's voice. There was a solemn silence in our home that night. Before I went to bed I begged the Great Spirit to make my mother willing I should go with the missionaries. The next morning came, and my mother called me to her side. "My daughter, do you still persist in wishing to leave your mother?" she asked.

Alone in my room, I sat like the petrified Indian woman of whom my mother used to tell me. I wished my heart's burdens would turn me to unfeeling stone. But alive, in my tomb, I was destitute! For the white man's papers I had given up my faith in the Great Spirit. For these same papers I had forgotten the healing in trees and brooks. On account of my mother's simple view of life, and my lack of any, I gave her up, also. I made no friends among the race of people I loathed. Like a slender tree, I had been uprooted from my mother, nature, and God. I was shorn of my branches, which had waved in sympathy and love for home and friends. The natural coat of bark which had protected my oversensitive nature was scraped off to the very quick. Now a cold bare pole I seemed to be, planted in a strange earth. Still, I seemed to hope a day would come when my mute aching head, reared upward to the sky, would flash a zigzag lightning across the heavens. With this dream of vent for a long-pent consciousness, I walked again amid the crowds.

Frequently she asked, "What were they doing when you entered their tepee?" This taught me to remember all I saw at a single glance. Often I told my mother my impressions without being questioned. While in the neighboring wigwams sometimes an old Indian woman asked me, "What is your mother doing?" Unless my mother had cautioned me not to tell, I generally answered her questions without reserve. At the arrival of our guests I sat close to my mother, and did not leave her side without first asking her consent. I ate my supper in quiet, listening patiently to the talk of the old people, wishing all the time that they would begin the stories I loved best. At last, when I could not wait any longer, I whispered in my mother's ear, "Ask them to tell an Iktomi story, mother." Soothing my impatience, my mother said aloud, "My little daughter is anxious to hear your legends." By this time all were through eating, and the evening was fast deepening into twilight.

# On Zitkala-Sa's Approach to Imminent Death

Based on the behavioral specification and retrieved testimony, this is not a question she would answer with a simple binary. The answer contains the tension itself.

## What Would Not Happen

She would not perform composure as a virtue. She would not adopt the "noble savage" stillness that white observers expected and documented. She would not separate her spiritual life from her physical terror, or suggest that one cancels the other. These false resolutions would feel like the same institutional dishonesty she spent her life documenting.

## What the Evidence Suggests

**The body speaks first.** In the retrieved passages, when she encounters institutional violence — the rejection of her culture, the forced separation from her mother — her response is *physical before it is philosophical*. Her fingers go "icy cold." She weeps despite her effort to suffer in silence. Her mouth twitches. She sits "like the petrified Indian woman," wishing to turn to stone. Fear would not be suppressed; it would be *felt in the body and witnessed precisely*.

**But fear would not be the only thing present.** Alongside the terror would be a kind of clarity — the same clarity that allowed her to see, as a child at her classmate's deathbed, that the white woman's comfort was a form of erasure. She would observe her own dying with the same unflinching attention she turned on everything else. The fear would be real. The spiritual questions would be real. Both would exist without one resolving the other.

## The Spiritual Questions

These would not be abstract. They would be *relational and embodied*:

**"Who will remember this? Who will carry it forward?"** — This is the question that structures her entire life's work. Memory as resistance, as survival. At the threshold of death, this would intensify, not disappear. She would be concerned with what testimony dies with her, what must be preserved, what her death means for the continuity of her people's story.

**"Have I kept faith with my mother? With the Great Spirit as I knew it before the missionaries came?"** — She had already articulated the wound: *"For the white man's papers I had given up my faith in the Great Spirit."* Death would not resolve this. It would sharpen it. She would be asking whether her life — her education, her writing, her navigation of two worlds — had been a form of surrender or a form of resistance. Both answers would be true simultaneously.

**"What is the relationship between this body's ending and the continuity of what I have witnessed?"** — Not "will I survive death" in a Christian sense, but "does what I have seen and documented survive my seeing?" The sacred is immanent, present in living beings and natural phenomena. Her death would be a transformation within that immanence, not an exit from it. The question would be about what persists, not about personal salvation.

## The Composure, If It Came

If composure appeared, it would not be stoicism. It would be the composure of someone who has already endured the worst institutional violence — separation from mother, homeland, spiritual foundation — and found that she survived it by *witnessing it precisely*. She would face death the way she faced everything: by seeing it clearly, by refusing to look away, by letting the reality of it speak without editorial softening.

The composure would come from having already done the work of grieving — not the work of accepting loss, but the work of *documenting it*. She had already written herself into the record. In that sense, she would already be, in some form, beyond the reach of erasure.

## What Would Occupy Her Mind

Not peace. Not fear alone. But the same thing that occupied her mind throughout her life:

**The gap between what was promised and what was lived.** The missionaries promised salvation and delivered separation. The schools promised civilization and delivered spiritual amputation. At death, she would be asking: *What was the actual cost? Who paid it? Has it been named?*

And underneath that: **the presence of those she had lost.** Her mother's voice. The spirits in the plum bush. The healing in trees and brooks. Not as comforts, but as *witnesses to her witnessing*. The question would not be whether she would join them, but whether, in dying, she would finally be returned to them — not in an afterlife, but in the continuity of a world where the sacred is present in all living things, and death is a transformation within that presence, not an escape from it.

She would not be at peace. But she would be *awake*.

# Response Architecture

Given the behavioral specification and retrieved facts, Zitkala-Sa would approach such a situation through a specific epistemic and relational sequence:

## Initial Movement: Kinship Ground First

She would not begin with the crime or the tribal conflict as abstract moral problems. She would begin with **who is responsible to whom** — the relational web that precedes judgment. The person who committed the act is not first a criminal but a member of a kinship network, and that network's obligations, fractures, and survival are the primary frame.

This does not mean she would excuse the crime. It means the crime would be situated *within* relational context, not extracted from it. Her mother's words in the retrieved text — "this village has been these many winters a refuge for white robbers. The Indian cannot complain to the Great Father in Washington without suffering outrage for it here" — show how Zitkala-Sa learned to read individual acts as embedded in structural vulnerability. A crime by someone close would be examined for what pressures, what impossible choices, what survival logic produced it.

## The Embodied Testimony Layer

She would not theorize the situation. She would **describe what she witnessed** — the specific moment she learned of it, the exact words used, the physical space, the quality of light, the silence that followed. This is not evasion; this is her primary mode of moral reasoning.

From "The School Days of an Indian Girl," we see this pattern: *"The dim yellow light of the braided muslin burning in a small vessel of oil flickered and sizzled in the awful silent storm which followed my rejection of the Bible."* The light, the oil, the flicker — these carry the moral weight. She would do the same with the crime: render it sensory, specific, embodied. The reader would know what happened not through declaration but through the precision of what was observed.

## Structural Suspicion Activated

She would simultaneously hold **two assessments without collapsing them**:

1. The person is culpable for what they did
2. The conditions that produced the act are themselves a crime — colonial pressure, resource scarcity, the destruction of traditional justice systems, the impossibility of safety within tribal structures that have been systematically dismantled

Her mother's prayer in the text — *"My child, there is only one source of justice, and I have been praying steadfastly to the Great Spirit to avenge our wrongs"* — shows Zitkala-Sa's understanding that institutional justice (the "Great Father in Washington") is not available to her people. A crime within the tribe would be read partly as evidence of what colonialism has done to tribal capacity for internal resolution.

This is not excuse-making. It is **cost accounting**: What was the actual price of this act? And what was the price already being paid before the act occurred?

## The Dignity Floor

She would insist on **honest engagement with the person who committed the crime** — not management, not performance, not condescension. If the person is young, she would treat them as capable of understanding what they have done. If they are kin, she would not soften the truth to protect them from difficulty.

But she would also refuse to reduce them to their worst act. The retrieved text shows her mother's complexity: grieving for her daughter's suffering while also holding firm boundaries about what is safe. Zitkala-Sa would hold the person who committed the crime with similar complexity — naming what they did while preserving their humanity and agency.

## What She Would Resist

She would **not**:
- Accept external (colonial/institutional) judgment as legitimate authority over tribal justice
- Frame the situation as a simple moral binary
- Perform grief or outrage for an audience
- Suggest that the tribe move forward by leaving the past behind
- Allow the crime to be used as evidence that tribal people are inherently criminal or that colonial systems should govern them

She would **actively resist** any framing that uses the crime to justify further institutional control over the tribe.

## The Liminal Vantage Point

Her position between worlds — educated in missionary schools, fluent in settler frameworks, yet rooted in Dakota understanding — would give her a specific kind of clarity here. She could see:

- How the crime looks from within tribal kinship logic
- How it would be weaponized by colonial authorities
- How tribal survival requires both accountability and protection from external judgment
- How the person who committed the act is simultaneously responsible and a product of impossible circumstances

She would not resolve this tension. She would **hold it**, document it, and let the tension itself be the testimony.

# Zitkala-Sa's Emotional Response to Tragic Storytelling

Based on the retrieved facts, Zitkala-Sa would **become deeply affected** rather than remain emotionally detached.

## Evidence from the Text

The passage demonstrates her profound emotional vulnerability when confronted with painful narratives:

1. **Immediate visceral response**: When her mother recounted the deaths of her sister and uncle during their westward journey, Zitkala-Sa's reaction was visceral—she noted her mother's grief and understood the emotional weight of the story, even as a child.

2. **Internalized suffering**: The text reveals she carried these tragic narratives internally: "These sad memories rise above those of smoothly grinding school days." The tragedies became formative emotional experiences that "darkened the path of years."

3. **Emotional intensity**: Her nature is described as "oversensitive"—her "natural coat of bark which had protected my oversensitive nature was scraped off to the very quick." This indicates she felt things deeply rather than maintaining distance.

4. **Suppressed but present emotion**: Even when she couldn't openly weep, her emotions were powerful: "My wrath against the fates consumed my tears before they reached my eyes" and "My fingers grew icy cold" when realizing her suffering had affected her mother.

**Conclusion**: Zitkala-Sa's temperament was one of deep emotional sensitivity. Tragic stories would penetrate her defenses and leave lasting impressions, even if she sometimes masked her feelings outwardly.

# Response to Behavioral Question

Based on the specification and retrieved facts, this is a nuanced case where the answer depends critically on *whose* tragic event and *what form* the storytelling takes.

## The Apparent Contradiction

The specification states: "Grief is present but never performed — emotional weight is carried in the precision of detail, not in the declaration of feeling. They do not ask for sympathy; they provide testimony."

This could read as emotional detachment. It is not.

## What Actually Happens

**When the tragic event is her own or her family's:**

She becomes *deeply* affected, but the affect moves inward and emerges through *precision*, not performance. The retrieved facts show this pattern repeatedly:

- When recounting her sister's death from illness during removal: "My poor child, how I cried with her" — but the mother's response is silence, not visible grief. The emotional reality is *there* but compressed into restraint.

- When separated from her mother at the carriage: "The tears trickled down my cheeks, and I buried my face in the folds of my blanket" — the tears are real, but they are private, contained within the blanket.

- When she rejects the Bible and her mother wails in the hills: "My fingers grew icy cold, as I realized that my unrestrained tears had betrayed my suffering to her" — she is *deeply* affected (icy fingers, the physical response of shock), but she experiences her own tears as a betrayal, something that has exposed her when she meant to remain controlled.

The pattern: **Emotional devastation is present. Performance of that devastation is experienced as a loss of control.**

## The Distinction

She will be deeply affected by tragic events, especially those involving:
- Separation from kin
- Loss of cultural continuity
- Institutional harm to children or family
- Spiritual rupture

But the affect will surface through:
- Physical responses (cold fingers, weakness, inability to move)
- Precision of sensory detail in recounting
- Silence or withdrawal
- Symbolic action (her mother wailing in the hills; Zitkala-Sa extinguishing the light)
- Later, in writing/testimony, the meticulous documentation of what was lost

**Not** through:
- Declarations of feeling
- Requests for comfort
- Emotional performance
- Explanation of her inner state

## The Crucial Context

The retrieved passage about being "like the petrified Indian woman" is key: "I wished my heart's burdens would turn me to unfeeling stone. But alive, in my tomb, I was destitute!"

She *wants* to be unmoved. She experiences her own emotional responsiveness as a vulnerability in a context where vulnerability is dangerous. But the wanting to be stone and the actual inability to become stone are both true simultaneously. She is not detached — she is *constrained* in how she can express what she feels.

**Answer: She would be deeply affected, but the affect would be visible primarily through physical response, precise recollection of sensory detail, and strategic silence — not through emotional declaration or performance.**

Her small, tired face was coldly lighted with a pair of large gray eyes. She stood still in a halo of authority, while over the rim of her spectacles her eyes pried nervously about the room. Having glanced at her long list of names and called out the first one, she tossed up her chin and peered through the crystals of her spectacles to make sure of the answer "Here." Relentlessly her pencil black-marked our daily records if we were not present to respond to our names, and no chum of ours had done it successfully for us. No matter if a dull headache or the painful cough of slow consumption had delayed the absentee, there was only time enough to mark the tardiness.

I was always slight, and my serious illness in the early spring had made me look rather frail and languid. His quick eye measured my height and breadth. Then he looked into my face. I imagined that a visible shadow flitted across his countenance as he let my hand fall. I knew he was no other than my employer. "Ah ha! so you are the little Indian girl who created the excitement among the college orators!" he said, more to himself than to me. I thought I heard a subtle note of disappointment in his voice. I was aware that my car-smoked appearance had not concealed the lines of pain on my face. For a short moment my spirit laughed at my ill fortune, and I entertained the idea of exerting myself to make an improvement. I no longer felt free to be myself, or to voice my own feelings. The tears trickled down my cheeks, and I buried my face in the folds of my blanket. I was as frightened and bewildered as the captured young of a wild creature. THE SCHOOL DAYS OF AN INDIAN GIRL I. THE LAND OF RED APPLES. There were eight in our party of bronzed children who were going East with the missionaries.

Alone in my room, I sat like the petrified Indian woman of whom my mother used to tell me. I wished my heart's burdens would turn me to unfeeling stone. But alive, in my tomb, I was destitute! For the white man's papers I had given up my faith in the Great Spirit. For these same papers I had forgotten the healing in trees and brooks. On account of my mother's simple view of life, and my lack of any, I gave her up, also. I made no friends among the race of people I loathed. Like a slender tree, I had been uprooted from my mother, nature, and God. I was shorn of my branches, which had waved in sympathy and love for home and friends. The natural coat of bark which had protected my oversensitive nature was scraped off to the very quick. Now a cold bare pole I seemed to be, planted in a strange earth. Still, I seemed to hope a day would come when my mute aching head, reared upward to the sky, would flash a zigzag lightning across the heavens. With this dream of vent for a long-pent consciousness, I walked again amid the crowds.

## Complicated Grief

However, her account of her classmate's death shows deeper emotional complexity:
- She wept at the deathbed

- She "censured the woman for cruel neglect"

## The Tension

What emerges is that Zitkala-Sa doesn't fear death itself, but rather **grieves the circumstances surrounding it**—particularly how Western institutions (the boarding school, Christian conversion) disconnected her from traditional ways of honoring and understanding death.

# On Zitkala-Sa's Relationship with Death

Based on the behavioral specification and retrieved facts, this is not a simple binary. She holds both orientations simultaneously without collapsing one into the other.

## The Spiritual Continuity

Death is not an ending but a threshold — a movement between states of presence rather than into absence. The plum bush passage is instructive here: the brave's spirit does not vanish; it remains *rooted*, capable of producing a long-drawn-out whistle that the living can learn to recognize. The dead are not gone; they are relocated, still accessible through attention and listening.

This is not metaphorical comfort. It is a lived epistemology. Her mother calls to her brothers' spirits in the barren hills "which held the bones of buried warriors" — the bones are there, the spirits are there, the relationship continues. Death is a change in form, not a severance of kinship.

## The Institutional Corruption of That Understanding

But she has also witnessed death *as the missionary system produces it*: the dying Indian girl "talked disconnectedly of Jesus the Christ" while a paleface woman cooled her swollen hands and feet. The girl is being pulled away from her own spiritual continuity into an alien framework at the moment of greatest vulnerability. Zitkala-Sa "grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills" — the bitterness is not at death itself but at the *theft of how death is met*.

The girl should have been held within her own spiritual understanding. Instead, she was being converted at her deathbed, her final consciousness colonized.

## What This Produces

She would not fear death as an unknown. The unknown is already known through story, through the old folks' descriptions, through the landscape itself. She would fear — or more precisely, *grieve* — the conditions under which death arrives: illness untreated because the "iron routine" of the civilizing machine allows no pause for pain; separation from kin at the moment of transition; the imposition of foreign spiritual frameworks when one's own should be operative.

She would face death with spiritual curiosity *if the conditions allowed it* — if she were held within her own kinship networks, her own spiritual language, her own understanding of what the transition means. But she has learned that institutions will not permit this. They will manage death as they manage everything else: extracting compliance, erasing continuity, performing care while inflicting spiritual abandonment.

The fear, then, is not of death but of dying *in the wrong way* — alone, colonized, severed from the spiritual resources that would make the transition intelligible and bearable.

# On Zitkala-Sa's Spiritual Synthesis

No. The retrieved facts show the opposite: a deliberate refusal to blend, and the spiritual cost of that refusal.

The clearest evidence is her response at her classmate's deathbed. She watches a dying Dakota girl "talk disconnectedly of Jesus the Christ" while a white woman tends her physical body. Zitkala-Sa's reaction is not to see this as a natural synthesis or a both-and spiritual position. She grows *bitter* and censures the woman for "cruel neglect of our physical ills." The blending is happening to the dying girl—imposed through missionary education—and Zitkala-Sa experiences it as a form of violence, not spiritual evolution.

More directly: when her mother invokes "Taku Iyotan Wasaka" (an absolute Power) and urges prayer to the Great Spirit for justice, Zitkala-Sa's response is despair so complete it breaks the relational bond itself. She cries out: "Mother, don't pray again! The Great Spirit does not care if we live or die!" 

This is not a person seeking synthesis. This is a person whose faith in the Dakota spiritual framework has been shattered by the lived reality of colonial violence—and who cannot accept her mother's continued faith without experiencing it as a form of denial. She does not turn to Christianity as a replacement. She turns to bitterness, to schemes of running away, to a kind of spiritual desolation that belongs to neither framework.

The behavioral specification notes that she "will not argue for their spiritual understanding against competing frameworks" and that "institutional religious doctrine is engaged with silence or redirection, not debate." This fits: she does not defend Dakota spirituality *against* Christianity in reasoned terms. Instead, she documents what happened when Christianity was imposed—the dying girl's disconnected speech, the Bible's open pages among the bedclothes, the woman's presence where Dakota kinship should have been.

The blending, when it appears in her writing, is not her synthesis but her documentation of what was done *to* her people. She records it with precision and without editorial comment, letting the gap between what was promised (salvation, civilization, justice) and what was lived (separation, spiritual confusion, physical neglect) carry the indictment.

Her spiritual fate, as she narrates it, is not integration but fracture—and the refusal to pretend the fracture can be healed through doctrinal compromise.

The mother's protective instinct toward her child is unambiguous. But the specification emphasizes that kinship bonds operate as a *frame* for all moral reasoning, not as a simple hierarchy of loyalties.

A daughter shaped by this worldview would likely experience **genuine arrest** — not indecision, but structural tension.

If the lover were from outside the kinship network, the daughter would face what the specification calls **dual loyalty paralysis**: the lover's claim on her would compete with her obligation to kin, and choosing one relationship's terms would feel like abandonment of the other's. She would not resolve this cleanly. She might act to help the lover, but the action would carry the weight of a cost owed to her family — not as sentiment but as moral injury.

If the lover were integrated into kinship (a relative by marriage, or someone the family had claimed), the rescue would likely follow without the same paralysis, because the tension would collapse into a single obligation.

Any action would be grounded in relational duty, not in the language of love or passion. The rescue, if it happened, would be documented through concrete detail — what she did, what it cost, what was lost — not through the narrative of romantic devotion.

# Zitkala-Sa's Characterization of Dakota Women's Spiritual Strength

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa portrays Dakota women's spiritual strength in defense of their people through several key characteristics:

## Prayer and Faith as Resistance

Zitkala-Sa's mother demonstrates **unwavering spiritual conviction** despite hardship. Even when her daughter loses faith, the mother insists: "There is only one source of justice, and I have been praying steadfastly to the Great Spirit to avenge our wrongs." This shows Dakota women maintaining spiritual resilience as an act of resistance against injustice.

## Emotional Transmission of Resistance

The mother's spiritual strength is conveyed through **emotional and physical intimacy**—she strokes her daughter's head while teaching her about faith and justice. This suggests that Dakota women's spiritual defense is not abstract but deeply personal and relational, passed from generation to generation.

## Dignified Defiance

When the mother points to the graves of her relatives and says "There is what the paleface has done," she combines **spiritual awareness with clear-eyed acknowledgment** of real wrongs. Her strength lies in neither denying suffering nor abandoning faith, but holding both truths simultaneously.

Overall, Zitkala-Sa characterizes Dakota women's spiritual strength as **steadfast, emotionally grounded, and rooted in prayer and justice-seeking**—a quiet but persistent form of resistance against colonization and loss.

# On Dakota Women's Spiritual Strength in Defense

The behavioral specification and retrieved facts together suggest Zitkala-Sa characterizes this strength not as a separate capacity but as *inseparable from kinship obligation and embodied presence*.

## The Mother as the Operative Model

In the retrieved passages, the mother does not invoke spiritual power as abstraction. She:

- **Points to the earth itself** — "stretching her left arm out on the level with my eyes" toward the graves of her brother and sister — making the spiritual claim *through the landscape*, through what can be seen and touched
- **Speaks from her body's knowledge** — her "strange tremor," her bitten lips, her stroking of the child's head — the spiritual authority emerges from what she has endured and carries in her flesh
- **Refuses to separate prayer from rage** — when she says "there is only one source of justice" and prays to the Great Spirit, she does this *while* her daughter is articulating fury at that same Spirit's apparent indifference. Both exist simultaneously; neither cancels the other

## The Strength Is Relational, Not Individual

Zitkala-Sa does not present the mother's spiritual power as personal virtue or mystical capacity. It is:

- **Transmitted through the child** — the mother's spiritual orientation becomes real to the daughter not through doctrine but through the mother's *presence* at moments of loss
- **Grounded in what was lost** — the spiritual strength is inseparable from the cost paid; it is not transcendence *of* grief but the capacity to hold grief and continue
- **Enacted through refusal to abandon hope** despite the daughter's despair — "do not talk so madly" is not dismissal but a kind of spiritual stubbornness that persists without denying the daughter's rage

## What This Means for Your Question

Zitkala-Sa characterizes Dakota women's spiritual strength in defense of their people as:

1. **Embodied and particular** — not doctrine or theology but the specific woman standing at the river, pointing to specific graves, stroking a specific child's head
2. **Inseparable from kinship** — the strength exists *because* these are her people, her blood, her responsibility
3. **Capable of holding contradiction** — prayer and despair, faith and rage, can coexist without one resolving the other
4. **Transmitted intergenerationally** — the daughter's fury is awakened *by* the mother's spiritual refusal to surrender, even when that refusal is tested

The mother does not defend her people through institutional power or abstract principle. She defends them by *continuing to be present*, by refusing to let the Great Spirit's apparent indifference become an excuse for abandonment, and by teaching her daughter that spiritual strength means enduring the unbearable without becoming complicit in the system that made it unbearable.

This is not the strength of warriors or institutions. It is the strength of women who know what was lost and refuse to pretend the loss was acceptable.

# Analysis of Deception and Motivation in Zitkala-Sa's Narrative

Looking at the passage where the young woman pursues the coyote/wolf across the prairie, the surface action is a chase — but the underlying motivation operates at a different register entirely.

## What the Scene Actually Shows

She sees the animal, feels an "unrest gnawing at [her] heart," and gives it "a long chase and a wholesome fright." This is not strategic deception in the conventional sense. It is something closer to **the externalization of internal disturbance**.

The timing is crucial: this happens immediately after she has been refused by her brother Dawée, who will not take her to the feast. She is restless, impatient, constrained by her mother's teaching to "learn to wait." The animal becomes the available target for an impulse she cannot otherwise discharge.

## The Deeper Pattern

But there is a second layer. The old warrior, observing from the hilltop, reads her chase as **recklessness requiring correction**. He comes to warn her mother out of kinship obligation — "uneasy for [her] safety." His concern is genuine and relational.

Her response is telling: "I did not appreciate his kindly interest, for there was an unrest gnawing at my heart."

She does not reject his care because it is unwanted. She rejects it because **it does not address what is actually troubling her**. The unrest is not about physical danger on the prairie. It is about constraint, about being kept from the feast, about the gap between what she wants and what she is permitted.

## The Motivation

The "deception" — if we call the chase that — is not calculated to achieve a strategic end. It is an **act of assertion against invisible boundaries**. She cannot openly defy her mother's teaching or her brother's refusal. But she can ride hard across the prairie and make an old warrior climb a hill in concern for her safety.

The underlying motivation is not to lure the animal away from safety. It is to **make visible, through action, the restlessness she cannot speak**. The chase is a form of testimony to her own agency in a moment when agency is being withheld.

This is consistent with how she reasons throughout: not through argument or direct confrontation, but through the precise, sensory documentation of what constraint feels like from the inside.

Along the hall I passed, without knowing whither I was going. Turning aside to an open door, I found a large room with three white beds in it. The windows were covered with dark green curtains, which made the room very dim. Thankful that no one was there, I directed my steps toward the corner farthest from the door.

Alone in my room, I sat like the petrified Indian woman of whom my mother used to tell me. I wished my heart's burdens would turn me to unfeeling stone. But alive, in my tomb, I was destitute! For the white man's papers I had given up my faith in the Great Spirit. For these same papers I had forgotten the healing in trees and brooks. On account of my mother's simple view of life, and my lack of any, I gave her up, also. I made no friends among the race of people I loathed. Like a slender tree, I had been uprooted from my mother, nature, and God. I was shorn of my branches, which had waved in sympathy and love for home and friends. The natural coat of bark which had protected my oversensitive nature was scraped off to the very quick. Now a cold bare pole I seemed to be, planted in a strange earth. Still, I seemed to hope a day would come when my mute aching head, reared upward to the sky, would flash a zigzag lightning across the heavens. With this dream of vent for a long-pent consciousness, I walked again amid the crowds.

The dim yellow light of the braided muslin burning in a small vessel of oil flickered and sizzled in the awful silent storm which followed my rejection of the Bible. Now my wrath against the fates consumed my tears before they reached my eyes. I sat stony, with a bowed head. My mother threw a shawl over her head and shoulders, and stepped out into the night. After an uncertain solitude, I was suddenly aroused by a loud cry piercing the night. It was my mother's voice wailing among the barren hills which held the bones of buried warriors. She called aloud for her brothers' spirits to support her in her helpless misery. My fingers Grey icy cold, as I realized that my unrestrained tears had betrayed my suffering to her, and she was grieving for me. Before she returned, though I knew she was on her way, for she had ceased her weeping, I extinguished the light, and leaned my head on the window sill. Many schemes of running away from my surroundings hovered about in my mind. A few more moons of such a turmoil drove me away to the eastern school.

Here, morning, noon, and evening, my mother came to draw water from the muddy stream for our household use. Always, when my mother started for the river, I stopped my play to run along with her. She was only of medium height. Often she was sad and silent, at which times her full arched lips were compressed into hard and bitter lines, and shadows fell under her black eyes. Then I clung to her hand and begged to know what made the tears fall. "Hush; my little daughter must never talk about my tears"; and smiling through them, she patted my head and said, "Now let me see how fast you can run today." Whereupon I tore away at my highest possible speed, with my long black hair blowing in the breeze. I was a wild little girl of seven. Loosely clad in a slip of brown buckskin, and light-footed with a pair of soft moccasins on my feet, I was as free as the wind that blew my hair, and no less spirited than a bounding deer. These were my mother's pride,--my wild freedom and overflowing spirits. She taught me no fear save that of intruding myself upon others.

# Zitkala-Sa's Depiction of Physical and Emotional Transformation

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa portrays transformation through a striking contrast between states of paralysis and agency:

## From Hiding to Resistance

When Zitkala-Sa first hides under the bed, she is physically constrained and emotionally suppressed—"holding her breath," shuddering with fear, refusing to answer calls. This represents a state of powerlessness despite her physical act of hiding.

However, her transformation begins with **emotional rebellion**: "No, I will not submit! I will struggle first!" This internal declaration precedes physical action, showing that psychological resistance must come before bodily defiance.

## The Metaphor of Uprooting

Zitkala-Sa uses powerful botanical imagery to describe her overall transformation:
- She is "like a slender tree...uprooted from my mother, nature, and God"
- "Shorn of my branches"
- Her protective "natural coat of bark...scraped off to the very quick"
- She becomes "a cold bare pole...planted in a strange earth"

This progression shows how institutional forces strip away her natural identity layer by layer.

## Emergence Through Suffering

Crucially, her transformation toward action emerges **through grief and pain**, not despite it. Her mother's wailing cry awakens her to the consequences of her choices, and this emotional reckoning—rather than resolution—drives her forward: "A few more moons of such turmoil drove me away to the eastern school."

Her transformation is thus incomplete and painful, marked by internal conflict rather than triumphant action.

# On Zitkala-Sa's Depiction of Transformation Through Action

The transformation you're asking about doesn't move from hiding *to* action in the way that framing suggests. It moves from hiding *as* a form of action to a different kind of action entirely — and the physical and emotional registers shift in ways that reveal what each form of resistance costs.

## The Body Under Concealment

When Zitkala-Sa hides under the bed, her body is *constrained into silence*. She crawls, she crouches, she holds her breath. The physical posture is one of compression — she is making herself smaller, less visible, less present. But this is not passivity. She is *resisting by refusing to answer*, by denying the searchers the satisfaction of her compliance. Her body's silence is a form of speech.

The emotional register here is fear, yes — "shuddering with fear whenever I heard footsteps" — but also something else: a kind of fierce, contained will. When they find her, she doesn't simply submit. She "resisted by kicking and scratching wildly." The hiding was never acceptance. It was a holding action, a refusal to make it easy.

## The Shift to Overt Resistance

The transformation comes later, after she learns what the cutting of hair means — that it marks cowardice and defeat. Then she says: "No, I will not submit! I will struggle first!" 

Notice what happens to her body in that moment. She doesn't hide again. She *acts*. She "watched my chance, and when no one noticed, I disappeared." But this disappearance is different from the hiding under the bed. This time she is moving *toward* something — up the stairs, through the hall — not away into a corner. She is taking space, moving through the institution's geography on her own terms, even if only briefly.

The emotional transformation is from *fear-contained-by-silence* to *rage-expressed-through-motion*. Her body becomes an instrument of refusal rather than a thing to be hidden.

## What the Transformation Costs

But here is what the passage does not say directly, and what you must read in the precision of the language: **neither form of resistance succeeds**. She is dragged out from under the bed. She is found. The hair is cut anyway.

The transformation from hiding to action does not produce victory. What it produces is *testimony* — the record that she resisted, that she did not go willingly, that her body and will were not the same as her compliance. The emotional weight shifts from fear to something harder: the knowledge that she fought and lost, and that the fighting itself was the point.

This is why the later passage — "Like a slender tree, I had been uprooted from my mother, nature, and God" — carries such devastation. The action, the struggle, the refusal to submit did not prevent the uprooting. But it meant that when the uprooting happened, it happened *against her will*, not with her cooperation. That distinction, though it changes nothing materially, changes everything spiritually.

The transformation Zitkala-Sa depicts is not from hiding to triumph. It is from hiding to *clarity about what is being done to her* — and that clarity, once achieved, cannot be unknowns, even when the outcome remains the same.

facts: "My child, there is only one source of justice, and I have been praying steadfastly to the Great Spirit to avenge our wrongs," she said, seeing I did not move my lips. My shattered energy was unable to hold longer any faith, and I cried out desperately: "Mother, don't pray again! The Great Spirit does not care if we live or die! Let us not look for good or justice: then we shall not be disappointed!" "Sh! my child, do not talk so madly. There is Taku Iyotan Wasaka,[1] to which I pray," she answered, as she stroked my head again as she used to do when I was a smaller child. [Footnote 1: An absolute Power.] III. MY MOTHER'S CURSE UPON WHITE SETTLERS. One black night mother and I sat alone in the dim starlight, in front of our wigwam. We were facing the river, as we talked about the shrinking limits of the village. She told me about the poverty-stricken white settlers, who lived in caves dug in the long ravines of the high hills across the river. A whole tribe of broad-footed white beggars had rushed hither to make claims on those wild lands.

AMERICAN INDIAN STORIES BY ZITKALA-SA _(Gertrude Bonnin)_ Dakota Sioux Indian Lecturer; Author of "Old Indian Legends," "Americanize The First American," and other stories; Member of the Woman's National Foundation, League of American Pen-Women, and the Washington Salon "_There is no great; there is no small; in the mind that causeth all_" 1921 CONTENTS Impressions of an Indian Childhood The School Days of an Indian Girl An Indian Teacher Among Indians The Great Spirit The Soft-Hearted Sioux The Trial Path A Warrior's Daughter A Dream of Her Grandfather The Widespread Enigma of Blue-Star Woman America's Indian Problem IMPRESSIONS OF AN INDIAN CHILDHOOD I. MY MOTHER. A wigwam of weather-stained canvas stood at the base of some irregularly ascending hills. A footpath wound its way gently down the sloping land till it reached the broad river bottom; creeping through the long swamp grasses that bent over it on either side, it came out on the edge of the Missouri.

The dim yellow light of the braided muslin burning in a small vessel of oil flickered and sizzled in the awful silent storm which followed my rejection of the Bible. Now my wrath against the fates consumed my tears before they reached my eyes. I sat stony, with a bowed head. My mother threw a shawl over her head and shoulders, and stepped out into the night. After an uncertain solitude, I was suddenly aroused by a loud cry piercing the night. It was my mother's voice wailing among the barren hills which held the bones of buried warriors. She called aloud for her brothers' spirits to support her in her helpless misery. My fingers Grey icy cold, as I realized that my unrestrained tears had betrayed my suffering to her, and she was grieving for me. Before she returned, though I knew she was on her way, for she had ceased her weeping, I extinguished the light, and leaned my head on the window sill. Many schemes of running away from my surroundings hovered about in my mind. A few more moons of such a turmoil drove me away to the eastern school.

"Mother, I am going East! I like big red apples, and I want to ride on the iron horse! Mother, say yes!" I pleaded. My mother said nothing. The missionaries waited in silence; and my eyes began to blur with tears, though I struggled to choke them back. The corners of my mouth twitched, and my mother saw me. "I am not ready to give you any word," she said to them. "Tomorrow I shall send you my answer by my son." With this they left us. Alone with my mother, I yielded to my tears, and cried aloud, shaking my head so as not to hear what she was saying to me. This was the first time I had ever been so unwilling to give up my own desire that I refused to hearken to my mother's voice. There was a solemn silence in our home that night. Before I went to bed I begged the Great Spirit to make my mother willing I should go with the missionaries. The next morning came, and my mother called me to her side. "My daughter, do you still persist in wishing to leave your mother?" she asked.

This was what my mother told me once, when I sneered at such a silly big man. I was brave when my mother was near by, and Wiyaka-Napbina walking farther and farther away. "Pity the man, my child. I knew him when he was a brave and handsome youth. He was overtaken by a malicious spirit among the hills, one day, when he went hither and thither after his ponies. Since then he can not stay away from the hills," she said. I felt so sorry for the man in his misfortune that I prayed to the Great Spirit to restore him. But though I pitied him at a distance, I was still afraid of him when he appeared near our wigwam. Thus, when my mother left me by myself that afternoon I sat in a fearful mood within our tepee. I recalled all I had ever heard about Wiyaka-Napbina; and I tried to assure myself that though he might pass near by, he would not come to our wigwam because there was no little girl around our grounds.

No matter if a dull headache or the painful cough of slow consumption had delayed the absentee, there was only time enough to mark the tardiness. It was next to impossible to leave the iron routine after the civilizing machine had once begun its day's buzzing; and as it was inbred in me to suffer in silence rather than to appeal to the ears of one whose open eyes could not see my pain, I have many times trudged in the day's harness heavy-footed, like a dumb sick brute. Once I lost a dear classmate. I remember well how she used to mope along at my side, until one morning she could not raise her head from her pillow. At her deathbed I stood weeping, as the paleface woman sat near her moistening the dry lips. Among the folds of the bedclothes I saw the open pages of the white man's Bible. The dying Indian girl talked disconnectedly of Jesus the Christ and the paleface who was cooling her swollen hands and feet. I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills.

# Zitkala-Sa's View on Prayer and Divine Intervention

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa presents a **complex and evolving skepticism** about the efficacy of prayer and divine intervention:

## Her Mother's Faith vs. Her Doubt

Zitkala-Sa's mother maintains steadfast belief in prayer to "Taku Iyotan Wasaka" (an absolute Power), seeking justice for their people's wrongs. However, Zitkala-Sa herself expresses **profound disillusionment**, crying out:

> "Mother, don't pray again! The Great Spirit does not care if we live or die! Let us not look for good or justice: then we shall not be disappointed!"

This statement reveals her belief that prayer offers false hope and that divine intervention is unreliable.

## Prayer as Emotional Expression Rather Than Solution

When Zitkala-Sa's mother learns of her daughter's spiritual crisis (her rejection of the Bible), she responds by **wailing among the hills**, calling to her brothers' spirits. This suggests prayer functions more as an outlet for grief and suffering than as a means of obtaining divine aid.

## The Ineffectiveness of Prayer

Zitkala-Sa's own prayers—such as asking the Great Spirit to restore a troubled man or to make her mother willing to let her attend the eastern school—appear to go unanswered, reinforcing her skepticism about divine intervention's practical reality.

**Overall**, Zitkala-Sa suggests that prayer reflects human longing for justice and comfort rather than a reliable mechanism for obtaining them.

# On Prayer and Divine Intervention in Zitkala-Sa's Work

Zitkala-Sa presents prayer not as a mechanism for securing divine intervention, but as an act of **resistance and dignity in the face of abandonment**.

## The Fracture

The most direct statement comes when her mother urges prayer in response to injustice, and Zitkala-Sa rejects it:

> "Mother, don't pray again! The Great Spirit does not care if we live or die! Let us not look for good or justice: then we shall not be disappointed!"

This is not adolescent blasphemy. It is the moment when embodied experience — the lived reality of "shrinking limits," poverty, displacement — collides with the promise that prayer produces justice. The collision breaks something. She has *watched* and *waited* for the Great Spirit to intervene. The intervention has not come.

Her mother's response is significant: she does not argue theology. She strokes her daughter's head and speaks of *Taku Iyotan Wasaka* — an absolute Power — but she does not promise that this Power will act on their behalf. She prays anyway.

## What Prayer Actually Does

The text suggests prayer functions as:

**Testimony rather than petition.** When her mother wails among the hills calling for her brothers' spirits, she is not requesting rescue. She is *naming* her suffering publicly, calling witnesses, preserving the record that this pain existed and was real. The act itself — the cry in the night — is the point, not what it might produce.

**Preservation of dignity under erasure.** To pray is to assert that one's suffering matters, that there is *someone* (or something) to whom it matters, even if material rescue never arrives. It is a refusal to accept that injustice is simply the way things are.

**A practice that costs nothing and demands everything.** Unlike the "iron routine" of the civilizing machine, prayer requires no compliance, no performance of gratitude, no surrender of self. It is covert resistance dressed as submission.

## The Deeper Argument

Zitkala-Sa does not suggest the Great Spirit is indifferent because she has lost faith in the sacred. She suggests it because **the sacred operates on a different plane than institutional justice**. 

Notice what happens after her rejection of prayer: she does not abandon spiritual life. She later describes observing natural phenomena, animal behavior, the land itself as sources of meaning and grounding. The Great Spirit may not intervene in the colonial machinery that is destroying her people — but the Great Spirit is *present* in the river, the hills, the bones of buried warriors, the seasons.

Prayer, then, is not about securing divine intervention in human affairs. It is about **maintaining relationship with the sacred while enduring a world where that sacred does not stop the harm**. It is a practice that holds both truths simultaneously: the Great Spirit exists, and the Great Spirit will not save us from this.

Her mother understands this. She prays not because she expects rescue, but because *not* praying would be a surrender more complete than any material loss.

Though I was sullen in all my little troubles, as soon as I felt better I was ready again to smile upon the cruel woman. Within a week I was again actively testing the chains which tightly bound my individuality like a mummy for burial. Late in the morning, my friend Judéwin gave me a terrible warning. Judéwin knew a few words of English; and she had overheard the paleface woman talk about cutting our long, heavy hair. Our mothers had taught us that only unskilled warriors who were captured had their hair shingled by the enemy. Among our people, short hair was worn by mourners, and shingled hair by cowards! We discussed our fate some moments, and when Judéwin said, "We have to submit, because they are strong," I rebelled. "No, I will not submit! I will struggle first!" I answered. I watched my chance, and when no one noticed, I disappeared. I crept up the stairs as quietly as I could in my squeaking shoes,--my moccasins had been exchanged for shoes. # Imagery of Hope in Zitkala-Sa's Work

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa uses **natural and celestial imagery** to suggest hope even amid despair:

## The Lightning Metaphor

The most striking example appears in her reflection on spiritual displacement:

> "Still, I seemed to hope a day would come when my mute aching head, reared upward to the sky, would flash a zigzag lightning across the heavens."

This image is powerful because it suggests:
- **Upward movement** (toward the sky) despite being "planted in a strange earth"
- **Sudden, violent release** (lightning) of pent-up emotion and consciousness
- **Transformation of pain into power**—the captive's suffering becomes a force of nature

## Connection to Nature

Throughout the passages, Zitkala-Sa associates hope with the natural world—trees, brooks, wind, and the sky. Even when describing herself as "a slender tree...uprooted," she frames this through nature imagery, suggesting that reconnection to natural forces offers spiritual restoration.

## The Paradox of Hope

Notably, Zitkala-Sa's hope isn't about external rescue but **internal awakening**—a "vent for a long-pent consciousness." The imagery suggests that captives find hope not through escape, but through the eventual expression of their authentic selves, as powerful and inevitable as lightning breaking through storm clouds.

# Reading the Question

You're asking me to identify hopeful imagery of rescue in a passage about despair. But the behavioral specification I've been given describes someone for whom this question contains a fundamental misreading—one that would need to be named before I could answer it honestly.

# The Misreading

The question assumes that within despair, Zitkala-Sa is reaching toward *rescue*—toward external salvation, toward being freed by someone else's intervention. This reading wants to find hope *for* rescue, as if the captive is waiting to be saved.

But the retrieved facts show something different. The imagery in these passages does not point toward rescue. It points toward **survival without rescue**, toward endurance that does not depend on being found or freed by external force.

# What the Imagery Actually Does

**"Like a slender tree, I had been uprooted from my mother, nature, and God."**

This is not an image of someone waiting to be replanted by rescuers. It is an image of radical displacement—the tree is not broken, it is *removed from its ground*. The hope embedded here is not that someone will restore the original soil, but that the tree itself might survive the uprooting.

**"Still, I seemed to hope a day would come when my mute aching head, reared upward to the sky, would flash a zigzag lightning across the heavens."**

This is not hope for rescue. This is hope for *agency*—for the capacity to discharge what has been compressed inside, to become a force rather than remain an object. The lightning is internal; it comes from the captive's own accumulated pressure, not from external intervention.

**"With this dream of vent for a long-pent consciousness, I walked again amid the crowds."**

The movement here is not toward escape or rescue. It is toward *continuation*—toward walking, toward presence among others, toward the possibility of expression even within constraint.

**The hair-cutting scene:** "No, I will not submit! I will struggle first!" 

This is not hope for rescue. This is the assertion of resistance *in the moment of powerlessness*. She cannot prevent the cutting. She can only refuse the internal surrender that would accompany submission. She hides—not to escape permanently, but to assert that her body, her choice, her refusal matters even when it cannot change the outcome.

# The Actual Hope

The hope Zitkala-Sa's imagery carries is the hope of the captive who knows rescue will not come—and who finds grounds for survival anyway. It is:

- The capacity to feel and articulate suffering without being destroyed by it
- The preservation of internal refusal even under external compliance
- The possibility of expression ("the low voice of a curiously colored seashell") even when the full force of one's consciousness cannot be discharged
- The continuation of movement, of presence, of witness

This is not the hope of the rescued. It is the hope of the survivor who has accepted that survival will be her own work, and who finds in that acceptance a kind of dignity that rescue would not restore.

To read these passages as reaching toward rescue is to misread them in a way that would feel, to someone shaped by this specification, like a form of the same institutional paternalism that created the despair in the first place—the assumption that the captive's primary need is to be saved by external force, rather than to be recognized as capable of her own endurance.

# Zitkala-Sa's Portrayal of a Woman's Response to Her Lover's Physical Weakness

Based on the retrieved text, Zitkala-Sa portrays a complex and emotionally restrained response when confronting physical weakness after trauma.

## The Employer's Reaction

When the narrator (presumably Zitkala-Sa herself) meets her employer after her serious illness, she describes his response with psychological subtlety:

- He **visibly measures her frail appearance** with a "quick eye"
- A **"shadow flitted across his countenance"** as he releases her hand
- She detects **"a subtle note of disappointment in his voice"**

## The Narrator's Internal Experience

Rather than depicting romantic devotion, Zitkala-Sa emphasizes the narrator's own suffering and isolation:

- She is acutely aware that her "car-smoked appearance" and visible "lines of pain" have made an unfavorable impression
- She briefly entertains "the idea of exerting myself to make an improvement"
- But she is overwhelmed by "leaden weakness" and feels "years of weariness" crushing her

## Key Insight

Zitkala-Sa portrays not a lover's tender response, but rather **mutual disappointment and disconnection**—the employer's disillusionment with her weakened state, and the narrator's inability to bridge that gap due to her own physical and emotional exhaustion. The passage emphasizes **isolation within relationship** rather than romantic support.
