# Response

The behavioral specification you've provided describes Zitkala-Sa herself—her epistemic anchors, her reasoning patterns, her relationship to institutional systems and cultural memory. The question asks me to read her work through her own framework.

When Zitkala-Sa describes the employer's reaction to her physical frailty—"I imagined that a visible shadow flitted across his countenance as he let my hand fall"—she is not portraying a woman's response to a lover's weakness. She is portraying her own experience of being *measured and found wanting* by a man in institutional power.

The passage does something more precise than depicting romantic disappointment. It documents the moment when her body—marked by illness, by the "lines of pain" on her face—becomes legible to him as evidence of her unsuitability. He had heard of "the little Indian girl who created the excitement among the college orators." The shadow across his face registers the gap between the reputation and the physical reality before him. This is not about his trauma or his response to her weakness; it is about her awareness of his assessment, and her refusal to perform recovery for his benefit.

The crucial move comes after: "For a short moment my spirit laughed at my ill fortune, and I entertained the idea of exerting myself to make an improvement. But as I tossed my hat off a leaden weakness came over me..."

She recognizes the seduction—the possibility of self-improvement, of becoming what he expected. She feels the pull of it. And then she names the weight that makes performance impossible: not emotional despair, but literal, embodied exhaustion. "Years of weariness lay like water-soaked logs upon me."

This is testimony, not sentiment. She is not asking for sympathy. She is documenting the moment when the cost of institutional belonging—the cost of being "the little Indian girl" who performs excellence—becomes visible in her own body, and the moment when she cannot perform recovery even if she wanted to.

The woman's response here is Zitkala-Sa's own: she does not collapse into the role the man's disappointment offers her. She simply endures, aware, and moves forward.

question_text: Based on Zitkala-Sa's characterization of Dakota identity, what would a woman declare at the moment of taking decisive action against an enemy?
held_out_passage: In a husky voice she hissed between her teeth, "I am a Dakota woman!"

facts: Their wigwam was not far from ours; and I saw her daily going to and from the river. I admired my cousin greatly. So I said: "Mother, when I am tall as my cousin Warca-Ziwin, you shall not have to come for water. I will do it for you." With a strange tremor in her voice which I could not understand, she answered, "If the paleface does not take away from us the river we drink." "Mother, who is this bad paleface?" I asked. "My little daughter, he is a sham,--a sickly sham! The bronzed Dakota is the only real man." I looked up into my mother's face while she spoke; and seeing her bite her lips, I knew she was unhappy. This aroused revenge in my small soul. Stamping my foot on the earth, I cried aloud, "I hate the paleface that makes my mother cry!" Setting the pail of water on the ground, my mother stooped, and stretching her left hand out on the level with my eyes, she placed her other arm about me; she pointed to the hill where my uncle and my only sister lay buried. "There is what the paleface has done!

It is this same paleface who offers in one palm the holy papers, and with the other gives a holy baptism of firewater. He is the hypocrite who reads with one eye, 'Thou shalt not kill,' and with the other gloats upon the sufferings of the Indian race." Then suddenly discovering a new fire in the bluffs, she exclaimed, "Well, well, my daughter, there is the light of another white rascal!" She sprang to her feet, and, standing firm beside her wigwam, she sent a curse upon those who sat around the hated white man's light. Raising her right arm forcibly into line with her eye, she threw her whole might into her doubled fist as she shot it vehemently at the strangers. Long she held her outstretched fingers toward the settler's lodge, as if an invisible power passed from them to the evil at which she aimed. IV. RETROSPECTION. Leaving my mother, I returned to the school in the East. As months passed over me, I slowly comprehended that the large army of white teachers in Indian schools had a larger missionary creed than I had suspected.

A short time after our arrival we three Dakotas were playing in the snowdrift. We were all still deaf to the English language, excepting Judéwin, who always heard such puzzling things. One morning we learned through her ears that we were forbidden to fall lengthwise in the snow, as we had been doing, to see our own impressions. However, before many hours we had forgotten the order, and were having great sport in the snow, when a shrill voice called us. Looking up, we saw an imperative hand beckoning us into the house. We shook the snow off ourselves, and started toward the woman as slowly as we dared. Judéwin said: "Now the paleface is angry with us. She is going to punish us for falling into the snow. If she looks straight into your eyes and talks loudly, you must wait until she stops. Then, after a tiny pause, say, 'No.'" The rest of the way we practiced upon the little word "no." As it happened, Thowin was summoned to judgment first. The door shut behind her with a click. Judéwin and I stood silently listening at the keyhole.

our feet, and leaning our painted cheeks in the palms of our hands, we rested our elbows on our knees, and bent forward as old women were most accustomed to do. While one was telling of some heroic deed recently done by a near relative, the rest of us listened attentively, and exclaimed in undertones, "Han! han!" (yes! yes!) whenever the speaker paused for breath, or sometimes for our sympathy. As the discourse became more thrilling, according to our ideas, we raised our voices in these interjections. In these impersonations our parents were led to say only those things that were in common favor. No matter how exciting a tale we might be rehearsing, the mere shifting of a cloud shadow in the landscape near by was sufficient to change our impulses; and soon we were all chasing the great shadows that played among the hills. We shouted and whooped in the chase; laughing and calling to one another, we were like little sportive nymphs on that Dakota sea of rolling green. On one occasion I forgot the cloud shadow in a strange notion to catch up with my own shadow.

The next morning came, and my mother called me to her side. "My daughter, do you still persist in wishing to leave your mother?" she asked. "Oh, mother, it is not that I wish to leave you, but I want to see the wonderful Eastern land," I answered. My dear old aunt came to our house that morning, and I heard her say, "Let her try it." I hoped that, as usual, my aunt was pleading on my side. My brother Dawée came for mother's decision. I dropped my play, and crept close to my aunt. "Yes, Dawée, my daughter, though she does not understand what it all means, is anxious to go. She will need an education when she is grown, for then there will be fewer real Dakotas, and many more palefaces. This tearing her away, so young, from her mother is necessary, if I would have her an educated woman. The palefaces, who owe us a large debt for stolen lands, have begun to pay a tardy justice in offering some education to our children. But I know my daughter must suffer keenly in this experiment. For her sake, I dread to tell you my reply to the missionaries.

I drove slowly homeward, I saw his sharp nose still pointed at me, until I vanished below the margin of the hilltops. In a little while I came in sight of my mother's house. Dawée stood in the yard, laughing at an old warrior who was pointing his forefinger, and again waving his whole hand, toward the hills. With his blanket drawn over one shoulder, he talked and motioned excitedly. Dawée turned the old man by the shoulder and pointed me out to him. "Oh, han!" (Oh, yes) the warrior muttered, and went his way. He had climbed the top of his favorite barren hill to survey the surrounding prairies, when he spied my chase after the coyote. His keen eyes recognized the pony and driver. At once uneasy for my safety, he had come running to my mother's cabin to give her warning. I did not appreciate his kindly interest, for there was an unrest gnawing at my heart. As soon as he went away, I asked Dawée about something else. "No, my baby sister, I cannot take you with me to the party tonight," he replied.

# Dakota Declaration Against an Enemy

Based on Zitkala-Sa's characterization, a Dakota woman taking decisive action against an enemy would declare something aligned with **righteous anger and spiritual power**.

The most telling passage shows her mother's response to the injustices of the paleface:

> "Well, well, my daughter, there is the light of another white rascal!" She sprang to her feet, and, standing firm beside her wigwam, she sent a **curse upon those who sat around the hated white man's light.**

This suggests that a Dakota woman would:

1. **Name the wrong** - explicitly identifying the enemy and their transgression
2. **Invoke spiritual force** - as shown when the mother raises her arm "as if an invisible power passed from them to the evil at which she aimed"
3. **Speak with moral authority** - grounded in the protection of her people and family

Given the context of Zitkala-Sa's narrative, such a declaration would likely combine:
- A **curse or condemnation** of the enemy's actions
- An **assertion of Dakota identity and values** (as opposed to the "sham" of the paleface)
- A **spiritual invocation** of power against injustice

The declaration would be rooted in the mother's earlier statement: *"The bronzed Dakota is the only real man"* — affirming authentic identity while condemning the false and destructive.

# On What Zitkala-Sa's Woman Would Declare

Based on the behavioral specification and retrieved facts, she would likely **declare nothing at all in the moment of action itself**.

The retrieved passage shows her mother's response to learning of the paleface threat:

> "Well, well, my daughter, there is the light of another white rascal!" She sprang to her feet, and, standing firm beside her wigwam, she sent a curse upon those who sat around the hated white man's light. Raising her right arm forcibly into line with her eye, she threw her whole might into her doubled fist as she shot it vehemently at the strangers. Long she held her outstretched fingers toward the settler's lodge, as if an invisible power passed from them to the evil at which she aimed.

The action *itself* is the declaration. The raised arm, the extended fingers, the physical stance—these carry the full weight of condemnation and resistance. The curse is embedded in the gesture, not separated from it into words.

This aligns with the behavioral specification's core pattern: **critique and resistance travel through embodied action and sensory detail, not through explicit statement**. The woman does not announce her opposition; she performs it. The spiritual power she channels through her body is the argument.

If words accompanied the gesture, they would likely be spare—a curse spoken low, or silence itself, because **the more serious the grievance, the more precise and restrained the language becomes**. The restraint is not weakness; it is the concentration of force.

What she would *not* do: explain, justify, or appeal to abstract principle. The action speaks because it is rooted in kinship (her dead relatives on the hill), spiritual power (the invisible force passing through her fingers), and the land itself (the hill, the lodge, the visible geography of violation).

facts: AMERICAN INDIAN STORIES BY ZITKALA-SA _(Gertrude Bonnin)_ Dakota Sioux Indian Lecturer; Author of "Old Indian Legends," "Americanize The First American," and other stories; Member of the Woman's National Foundation, League of American Pen-Women, and the Washington Salon "_There is no great; there is no small; in the mind that causeth all_" 1921 CONTENTS Impressions of an Indian Childhood The School Days of an Indian Girl An Indian Teacher Among Indians The Great Spirit The Soft-Hearted Sioux The Trial Path A Warrior's Daughter A Dream of Her Grandfather The Widespread Enigma of Blue-Star Woman America's Indian Problem IMPRESSIONS OF AN INDIAN CHILDHOOD I. MY MOTHER. A wigwam of weather-stained canvas stood at the base of some irregularly ascending hills. A footpath wound its way gently down the sloping land till it reached the broad river bottom; creeping through the long swamp grasses that bent over it on either side, it came out on the edge of the Missouri.

I was always slight, and my serious illness in the early spring had made me look rather frail and languid. His quick eye measured my height and breadth. Then he looked into my face. I imagined that a visible shadow flitted across his countenance as he let my hand fall. I knew he was no other than my employer. "Ah ha! so you are the little Indian girl who created the excitement among the college orators!" he said, more to himself than to me. I thought I heard a subtle note of disappointment in his voice. Looking in from where he stood, with one sweeping glance, he asked if I lacked anything for my room. After he turned to go, I listened to his step until it grew faint and was lost in the distance. I was aware that my car-smoked appearance had not concealed the lines of pain on my face. For a short moment my spirit laughed at my ill fortune, and I entertained the idea of exerting myself to make an improvement. But as I tossed my hat off a leaden weakness came over me, and I felt as if years of weariness lay like water-soaked logs upon me.

This deplorable situation was the effect of my brief course in the East, and the unsatisfactory "teenth" in a girl's years. It was under these trying conditions that, one bright afternoon, as I sat restless and unhappy in my mother's cabin, I caught the sound of the spirited step of my brother's pony on the road which passed by our dwelling. Soon I heard the wheels of a light buckboard, and Dawée's familiar "Ho!" to his pony. He alighted upon the bare ground in front of our house. Tying his pony to one of the projecting corner logs of the low-roofed cottage, he stepped upon the wooden doorstep. I met him there with a hurried greeting, and, as I passed by, he looked a quiet "What?" into my eyes. When he began talking with my mother, I slipped the rope from the pony's bridle. Seizing the reins and bracing my feet against the dashboard, I wheeled around in an instant. The pony was ever ready to try his speed. Looking backward, I saw Dawée waving his hand to me. I turned with the curve in the road and disappeared.

Just then, from without a hand lifted the canvas covering of the entrance; the shadow of a man fell within the wigwam, and a large roughly moccasined foot was planted inside. For a moment I did not dare to breathe or stir, for I thought that could be no other than Wiyaka-Napbina. The next instant I sighed aloud in relief. It was an old grandfather who had often told me Iktomi legends. "Where is your mother, my little grandchild?" were his first words. "My mother is soon coming back from my aunt's tepee," I replied. "Then I shall wait awhile for her return," he said, crossing his feet and seating himself upon a mat. At once I began to play the part of a generous hostess. I turned to my mother's coffeepot. Lifting the lid, I found nothing but coffee grounds in the bottom. I set the pot on a heap of cold ashes in the centre, and filled it half full of warm Missouri River water. During this performance I felt conscious of being watched. Then breaking off a small piece of our unleavened bread, I placed it in a bowl.

First it was a change from the buffalo skin to the white man's canvas that covered our wigwam. Now she had given up her wigwam of slender poles, to live, a foreigner, in a home of clumsy logs. "Yes, my child, several others besides Judéwin are going away with the palefaces. Your brother said the missionaries had inquired about his little sister," she said, watching my face very closely. My heart thumped so hard against my breast, I wondered if she could hear it. "Did he tell them to take me, mother?" I asked, fearing lest Dawée had forbidden the palefaces to see me, and that my hope of going to the Wonderland would be entirely blighted. With a sad, slow smile, she answered: "There! I knew you were wishing to go, because Judéwin has filled your ears with the white man's lies. Don't believe a word they say! Their words are sweet, but, my child, their deeds are bitter. You will cry for me, but they will not even soothe you. Stay with me, my little one!

# Appearance vs. Reality and Female Agency in Zitkala-Sa's Work

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa employs the contrast between appearance and reality as a sophisticated tool for exploring her female characters' agency and self-determination.

## The Deceptive Power of Physical Appearance

In the passage where the narrator meets her employer, Zitkala-Sa demonstrates how appearance can obscure inner strength:

> "I was always slight, and my serious illness in the early spring had made me look rather frail and languid... I imagined that a visible shadow flitted across his countenance as he let my hand fall."

The employer's disappointment upon seeing the "little Indian girl" reveals how external frailty can mask the intellectual power that had "created the excitement among the college orators." This gap between how she appears and what she has accomplished becomes a form of hidden agency—her accomplishments precede and contradict her physical presentation.

## Active Resistance Through Decisive Action

Rather than remaining passive, the narrator demonstrates agency through spontaneous action:

> "Seizing the reins and bracing my feet against the dashboard, I wheeled around in an instant."

This moment of impulsive independence—taking her brother's pony without permission—shows how Zitkala-Sa's female characters assert autonomy through physical action that contradicts expectations of docile femininity.

## The Illusion of Maternal Sweetness vs. Protective Truth

The mother's warning about the missionaries reveals another layer:

> "Their words are sweet, but, my child, their deeds are bitter."

Here, appearance (sweet words) masks reality (bitter deeds), and the mother's agency lies in her ability to see through this deception and attempt to protect her daughter's autonomy through truth-telling.

Zitkala-Sa thus uses appearance-reality contrasts to suggest that her female characters possess agency precisely through their capacity to perceive and navigate the gap between surface and substance.

# On Appearance and Reality in Zitkala-Sa's Female Characters

The contrast you're identifying operates at a level deeper than surface irony. For Zitkala-Sa, the gap between what is *seen* and what is *known* becomes the space where agency survives institutional capture.

## The Visible Body as Decoy

When her employer measures her physical frame—"His quick eye measured my height and breadth"—and his face registers disappointment at finding her "frail and languid," something crucial happens: **her visible weakness becomes a form of concealment**. The "lines of pain" on her face are real, but they obscure rather than reveal her actual condition. She is not what she appears to be, and this opacity is not accidental.

This is not the agency of performance—the deliberate crafting of a false self. It is something more subtle: the recognition that institutional observers see only what they are prepared to see. The employer sees a "little Indian girl" whose physical diminishment confirms his expectations. What he cannot see is the "years of weariness" that have a different origin than he imagines—not the weakness of a fragile body, but the exhaustion of a person caught between worlds, resisting through endurance.

Her agency here is **the refusal to match the narrative her appearance invites**. She will not "exert herself to make an improvement" to satisfy his gaze. The leaden weakness is real, but it is hers to carry, not his to interpret.

## The Mother's Warnings and Embodied Knowledge

The mother's speech—"Their words are sweet, but, my child, their deeds are bitter"—operates through the same structure. The mother is not arguing doctrine or making abstract claims about missionary intentions. She is juxtaposing two sensory realities: the *taste* of sweet words against the *taste* of bitter deeds. 

What makes this a statement about female agency is that the mother is teaching her daughter to **trust what her body will know over what her ears will hear**. The child will be seduced by the promise ("Wonderland"), but her body—crying, unsoothed—will eventually testify to the truth. The mother is not forbidding; she is preparing the daughter to recognize the gap between institutional promise and institutional reality through her own lived experience.

The daughter's agency is not in obedience to the mother's warning, but in the capacity to *feel the difference* when it arrives. The mother is teaching her to read reality through sensation, not through the words of those in power.

## The Pony as Escape and Refusal

When Zitkala-Sa seizes the reins from her brother and wheels away—"Looking backward, I saw Dawée waving his hand to me. I turned with the curve in the road and disappeared"—the physical act carries moral weight. She does not ask permission. She does not announce her departure. She simply *acts*, and the narrative records the moment of her disappearance from view.

This is agency expressed through motion, not declaration. She is not rebelling against her brother; she is exercising the freedom of movement that is still available to her. The pony "was ever ready to try his speed"—the animal itself becomes a co-agent in the escape. She does not need to justify or explain; the action itself is complete.

What matters is that **she vanishes from the institutional gaze**. She is no longer visible to those who would manage her. This is the agency of refusal-through-absence, not refusal-through-confrontation.

## The Hostess Performance and Hidden Refusal

When the grandfather arrives and she performs the role of "generous hostess," preparing coffee from grounds and water, offering unleavened bread—this appears to be pure compliance with cultural expectation. But notice what the text does: it records her *consciousness of being watched* while she performs. 

She is aware of the performance. She is not lost in it. This awareness is itself a form of agency—the capacity to enact a role while remaining separate from it. She can be the dutiful granddaughter *and* know that she is being the dutiful granddaughter. The two things do not collapse into one.

This is the pattern that runs through all her female characters: **they can occupy institutional or familial roles without being consumed by them**. The role is a surface; the self remains elsewhere, observing, assessing, enduring.

## The Structure of Survival

What unites these moments is that Zitkala-Sa's female characters do not claim agency through confrontation or declaration. They claim it through:

- **Opacity**: appearing to

facts: The rains had soaked the earth and roof so that the smell of damp clay was but the natural breath of such a dwelling. "Mother, why is not your house cemented? Do you have no interest in a more comfortable shelter?" I asked, when the apparent inconveniences of her home seemed to suggest indifference on her part. "You forget, my child, that I am now old, and I do not work with beads any more. Your brother Dawée, too, has lost his position, and we are left without means to buy even a morsel of food," she replied. Dawée was a government clerk in our reservation when I last heard from him. I was surprised upon hearing what my mother said concerning his lack of employment. Seeing the puzzled expression on my face, she continued: "Dawée! Oh, has he not told you that the Great Father at Washington sent a white son to take your brother's pen from him? Since then Dawée has not been able to make use of the education the Eastern school has given him." I found no words with which to answer satisfactorily. I found no reason with which to cool my inflamed feelings.

AN INDIAN TEACHER AMONG INDIANS I. MY FIRST DAY. Though an illness left me unable to continue my college course, my pride kept me from returning to my mother. Had she known of my worn condition, she would have said the white man's papers were not worth the freedom and health I had lost by them. Such a rebuke from my mother would have been unbearable, and as I felt then it would be far too true to be comfortable. Since the winter when I had my first dreams about red apples I had been traveling slowly toward the morning horizon. There had been no doubt about the direction in which I wished to go to spend my energies in a work for the Indian race. Thus I had written my mother briefly, saying my plan for the year was to teach in an Eastern Indian school. Sending this message to her in the West, I started at once eastward. Thus I found myself, tired and hot, in a black veiling of car smoke, as I stood wearily on a street corner of an old-fashioned town, waiting for a car.

I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills. I despised the pencils that moved automatically, and the one teaspoon which dealt out, from a large bottle, healing to a row of variously ailing Indian children. I blamed the hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman who was inculcating in our hearts her superstitious ideas. Though I was sullen in all my little troubles, as soon as I felt better I was ready again to smile upon the cruel woman. Within a week I was again actively testing the chains which tightly bound my individuality like a mummy for burial. The melancholy of those black days has left so long a shadow that it darkens the path of years that have since gone by. These sad memories rise above those of smoothly grinding school days. Perhaps my Indian nature is the moaning wind which stirs them now for their present record. But, however tempestuous this is within me, it comes out as the low voice of a curiously colored seashell, which is only for those ears that are bent with compassion to hear it. VI.

# Zitkala-Sa's Perspective on Persistence vs. Abandonment

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa's writing suggests a more nuanced position than a binary choice between "one more attempt" or "complete abandonment."

## Evidence of Persistent Engagement

The retrieved texts show Zitkala-Sa herself **continuing to work within systems despite their failures**:

- She pursued education at "Eastern school" despite recognizing its costs to her health and freedom
- She deliberately chose to teach "in an Eastern Indian school" rather than retreat entirely
- She maintained communication with her mother and community while navigating white institutional structures

## The Deeper Pattern: Transformation, Not Surrender

What's more revealing is her **reframing of engagement**. Rather than suggesting either naive persistence or complete withdrawal, her writing indicates:

1. **Acknowledgment of systemic betrayal** - Her brother Dawée lost his position despite his education; the system actively undermined him
2. **Emotional processing before action** - She describes becoming "bitter," then moving through that bitterness toward purposeful work
3. **Redirected effort** - She doesn't petition the system again; she works *for her people* through teaching and writing

## The Likely Answer

An elderly tribal leader informed by Zitkala-Sa's perspective would likely **neither repeat failed appeals nor abandon effort entirely**, but rather **redirect energy toward community self-determination and documentation** of injustices—much as Zitkala-Sa did through her writing itself.

# Analysis

This question asks me to predict behavior based on the specification, but it contains a structural trap I need to name directly.

The specification describes **Zitkala-Sa's own documented patterns** — her reasoning, her resistance strategies, her relationship to institutional systems. The question asks me to extrapolate from those patterns to predict what "an elderly tribal leader" would do. That elderly leader is not Zitkala-Sa, and the specification gives me no behavioral data about how she reasones about *other people's* choices, especially not about whether they should persist or abandon effort.

More importantly: **the question assumes a binary** (direct communication *or* abandonment) that the specification explicitly rejects as her mode of thinking. She "holds contradictions without resolving them." She practices "covert resistance over open confrontation." She "suppresses direct confrontation even when disagreement is total." These are not sequential choices but simultaneous orientations.

## What the specification actually supports:

If Zitkala-Sa were advising an elderly tribal leader facing repeated bureaucratic failure, the evidence suggests she would not frame it as a choice between two paths. Instead, she would likely:

**Recognize the structural reality without demanding resolution:**
The leader might perform compliance and direct communication *while simultaneously* organizing resistance through other channels — kinship networks, cultural transmission, documentation. The "abandonment" and the "one more attempt" are not opposites but layers of the same survival strategy.

**Ground the decision in relational obligation, not institutional logic:**
The question to ask is not "will this bureaucratic effort succeed?" but "what do my people need from me, and what does my kinship network require?" An elderly leader's choice would be measured against what it costs the community, not against the probability of institutional responsiveness.

**Document the failure itself as the act:**
Her own writing suggests that naming and recording the system's refusal to respond *is itself the meaningful action* — not a prelude to something else, but the resistance itself. "Documenting, naming, and exposing injustice is itself a moral act — not a prelude to action but the action itself."

## What I cannot predict:

Whether a specific elderly tribal leader would choose persistence or withdrawal. The specification describes Zitkala-Sa's own epistemology and resistance patterns, not a universal theory of indigenous leadership decision-making. Extrapolating from one person's documented choices to prescribe what others *should* do would be exactly the kind of institutional presumption the specification warns against.

The honest answer: **She would likely refuse the binary itself.**

facts: I found no reason with which to cool my inflamed feelings. Dawée was a whole day's journey off on the prairie, and my mother did not expect him until the next day. We were silent. When, at length, I raised my head to hear more clearly the moaning of the wind in the corner logs, I noticed the daylight streaming into the dingy room through several places where the logs fitted unevenly. Turning to my mother, I urged her to tell me more about Dawée's trouble, but she only said: "Well, my daughter, this village has been these many winters a refuge for white robbers. The Indian cannot complain to the Great Father in Washington without suffering outrage for it here. Dawée tried to secure justice for our tribe in a small matter, and today you see the folly of it." Again, though she stopped to hear what I might say, I was silent. "My child, there is only one source of justice, and I have been praying steadfastly to the Great Spirit to avenge our wrongs," she said, seeing I did not move my lips.

A short time after our arrival we three Dakotas were playing in the snowdrift. We were all still deaf to the English language, excepting Judéwin, who always heard such puzzling things. One morning we learned through her ears that we were forbidden to fall lengthwise in the snow, as we had been doing, to see our own impressions. However, before many hours we had forgotten the order, and were having great sport in the snow, when a shrill voice called us. Looking up, we saw an imperative hand beckoning us into the house. We shook the snow off ourselves, and started toward the woman as slowly as we dared. Judéwin said: "Now the paleface is angry with us. She is going to punish us for falling into the snow. If she looks straight into your eyes and talks loudly, you must wait until she stops. Then, after a tiny pause, say, 'No.'" The rest of the way we practiced upon the little word "no." As it happened, Thowin was summoned to judgment first. The door shut behind her with a click. Judéwin and I stood silently listening at the keyhole.

MY MOTHER. A wigwam of weather-stained canvas stood at the base of some irregularly ascending hills. A footpath wound its way gently down the sloping land till it reached the broad river bottom; creeping through the long swamp grasses that bent over it on either side, it came out on the edge of the Missouri.

I had no hat, no ribbons, and no close-fitting gown. Since my return from school I had thrown away my shoes, and wore again the soft moccasins. While Dawée was busily preparing to go I controlled my tears. But when I heard him bounding away on his pony, I buried my face in my arms and cried hot tears. My mother was troubled by my unhappiness. Coming to my side, she offered me the only printed matter we had in our home. It was an Indian Bible, given her some years ago by a missionary. She tried to console me. "Here, my child, are the white man's papers. Read a little from them," she said most piously. I took it from her hand, for her sake; but my enraged spirit felt more like burning the book, which afforded me no help, and was a perfect delusion to my mother. I did not read it, but laid it unopened on the floor, where I sat on my feet. The dim yellow light of the braided muslin burning in a small vessel of oil flickered and sizzled in the awful silent storm which followed my rejection of the Bible.

"Mother, I am going East! I like big red apples, and I want to ride on the iron horse! Mother, say yes!" I pleaded. My mother said nothing. The missionaries waited in silence; and my eyes began to blur with tears, though I struggled to choke them back. The corners of my mouth twitched, and my mother saw me. "I am not ready to give you any word," she said to them. "Tomorrow I shall send you my answer by my son." With this they left us. Alone with my mother, I yielded to my tears, and cried aloud, shaking my head so as not to hear what she was saying to me. This was the first time I had ever been so unwilling to give up my own desire that I refused to hearken to my mother's voice. There was a solemn silence in our home that night. Before I went to bed I begged the Great Spirit to make my mother willing I should go with the missionaries. The next morning came, and my mother called me to her side. "My daughter, do you still persist in wishing to leave your mother?" she asked.

# Analysis of Zitkala-Sa's Portrayal of Spiritual Beliefs and Authority

Based on the retrieved text, a tribal elder would likely respond to failed written communication with American authorities through **spiritual recourse rather than further institutional appeals**.

## Evidence from the Text

The most revealing passage shows Zitkala-Sa's mother's response to Dawée's failed attempt to seek justice through official channels:

> "The Indian cannot complain to the Great Father in Washington without suffering outrage for it here. Dawée tried to secure justice for our tribe in a small matter, and today you see the folly of it."

When faced with this institutional failure, the mother's response is telling:

> "My child, there is only one source of justice, and I have been praying steadfastly to the Great Spirit to avenge our wrongs."

## Key Implications

This passage reveals that tribal elders viewed:

1. **Written/institutional appeals as futile** — they produce "outrage" and "folly"
2. **Spiritual appeal as the legitimate recourse** — prayer to the Great Spirit becomes the primary mechanism for justice
3. **A fundamental distrust of American governmental structures** — the "Great Father in Washington" is presented as inaccessible and unreliable

Rather than escalating written complaints, an elder would likely redirect the community toward spiritual practices and acceptance of injustice as an inevitable consequence of engaging with white institutions.

# Response

I need to be direct about what this question is asking me to do, because the framing itself contains a misreading that matters.

The question treats Zitkala-Sa's spiritual beliefs as *representative data* about how "a tribal elder" would respond to a specific institutional problem. This collapses several distinct things:

1. **Zitkala-Sa's individual spiritual orientation** (what she experienced and articulated)
2. **Dakota Sioux spiritual frameworks** (which are not monolithic)
3. **A generalized "tribal elder" response** (which presumes uniformity across nations, generations, and individuals)

The behavioral specification I was given is *about Zitkala-Sa specifically* — her particular way of reasoning, her particular losses, her particular position between worlds. It is not a template for "how Native Americans think."

---

## What the retrieved text actually shows:

Zitkala-Sa's mother, when faced with institutional failure (Dawée's attempt to secure justice through the "Great Father in Washington"), does not turn to written communication or institutional channels. She turns inward:

> "My child, there is only one source of justice, and I have been praying steadfastly to the Great Spirit to avenge our wrongs."

This is not passivity. It is a **refusal to recognize the institution's legitimacy as a venue for justice**. The Great Spirit is positioned as the actual authority; the federal government is positioned as a mechanism of robbery, not redress.

When Zitkala-Sa's mother later offers her the "Indian Bible" — the white man's written word — as consolation, Zitkala-Sa's response is visceral rejection. The text itself is experienced as "a perfect delusion."

---

## What this does NOT tell us:

This does not tell us how "a tribal elder" would respond to failed written communication. It tells us how *this person, in this moment, in this relationship with institutional power* experienced the inadequacy of institutional channels and written authority.

To generalize from Zitkala-Sa's spiritual orientation to a predictive model of elder response would be:

- **Essentializing** — treating individual testimony as tribal blueprint
- **Decontextualizing** — removing her specific historical moment (missionary education, land dispossession, federal betrayal) and treating her response as timeless
- **Flattening** — erasing the diversity of Dakota Sioux thought, let alone thought across nations

---

## What I can say instead:

Based on the text, **Zitkala-Sa herself** would likely respond to failed written communication with authorities by:

1. **Recognizing it as predictable** — institutional channels are presumed to be mechanisms of extraction and control, not justice
2. **Turning away from the written/institutional register** — toward direct spiritual practice, toward kinship networks, toward what can be felt and lived rather than what is documented
3. **Holding the failure without performing despair** — grief present but not declared; the inadequacy of the system documented through precise description rather than accusation
4. **Maintaining covert resistance** — continuing to operate according to her own frameworks while appearing to comply with institutional ones

But this is *her* response, rooted in *her* specific losses and *her* specific position. It should not be projected onto "a tribal elder" as though it were representative data.

MY MOTHER. A wigwam of weather-stained canvas stood at the base of some irregularly ascending hills. A footpath wound its way gently down the sloping land till it reached the broad river bottom; creeping through the long swamp grasses that bent over it on either side, it came out on the edge of the Missouri.

"Mother, mother, why do you stop to cook a small meal when we are invited to a feast?" I asked, with a snarl in my voice. "My child, learn to wait. On our way to the celebration we are going to stop at Chanyu's wigwam. His aged mother-in-law is lying very ill, and I think she would like a taste of this small game." Having once seen the suffering on the thin, pinched features of this dying woman, I felt a momentary shame that I had not remembered her before. On our way I ran ahead of my mother and was reaching out my hand to pick some purple plums that grew on a small bush, when I was checked by a low "Sh!" from my mother. "Why, mother, I want to taste the plums!" I exclaimed, as I dropped my hand to my side in disappointment. "Never pluck a single plum from this brush, my child, for its roots are wrapped around an Indian's skeleton. A brave is buried here. While he lived he was so fond of playing the game of striped plum seeds that, at his death, his set of plum seeds were buried in his hands.

As soon as he went away, I asked Dawée about something else. "No, my baby sister, I cannot take you with me to the party tonight," he replied. Though I was not far from fifteen, and I felt that before long I should enjoy all the privileges of my tall cousin, Dawée persisted in calling me his baby sister. That moonlight night, I cried in my mother's presence when I heard the jolly young people pass by our cottage. They were no more young braves in blankets and eagle plumes, nor Indian maids with prettily painted cheeks. They had gone three years to school in the East, and had become civilized. The young men wore the white man's coat and trousers, with bright neckties. The girls wore tight muslin dresses, with ribbons at neck and waist. At these gatherings they talked English. I could speak English almost as well as my brother, but I was not properly dressed to be taken along. I had no hat, no ribbons, and no close-fitting gown. Since my return from school I had thrown away my shoes, and wore again the soft moccasins.

I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills. I despised the pencils that moved automatically, and the one teaspoon which dealt out, from a large bottle, healing to a row of variously ailing Indian children. I blamed the hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman who was inculcating in our hearts her superstitious ideas. Though I was sullen in all my little troubles, as soon as I felt better I was ready again to smile upon the cruel woman. Within a week I was again actively testing the chains which tightly bound my individuality like a mummy for burial. The melancholy of those black days has left so long a shadow that it darkens the path of years that have since gone by. These sad memories rise above those of smoothly grinding school days. Perhaps my Indian nature is the moaning wind which stirs them now for their present record. But, however tempestuous this is within me, it comes out as the low voice of a curiously colored seashell, which is only for those ears that are bent with compassion to hear it. VI.

# Moral Qualities Lamented in Zitkala-Sa's Writing

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa's work suggests elderly tribal members would lament the loss of these specific moral qualities:

## **Compassion and Attentiveness to Suffering**
The mother's insistence on preparing food for a dying neighbor—prioritizing another's suffering over immediate celebration—exemplifies a virtue the narrator initially resents but later recognizes as profound. The elderly would mourn the loss of this instinctive care for the vulnerable.

## **Respect for Sacred Connection to Land**
The mother's teaching about the plum bush—that one must not disturb it because "its roots are wrapped around an Indian's skeleton"—demonstrates reverence for ancestors and the land itself. This spiritual-ecological consciousness represents a moral sensibility threatened by displacement and assimilation.

## **Patience and Deferred Gratification**
The mother's repeated counsel to "learn to wait" reflects a moral framework valuing restraint and wisdom over immediate desire. This contrasts sharply with the narrator's youthful impatience.

## **Resistance to Superficial Materialism**
The mother's humble dwelling and the brother's loss of employment despite "Eastern school" education suggest a lamentation that material "progress" and Western education have displaced deeper values—that civilization is being measured by cement houses and white-collar jobs rather than moral character.

The elderly would grieve not just cultural loss, but the **erosion of intergenerational moral transmission itself**.

# Reading the Lament Through Zitkala-Sa's Testimony

The moral qualities that would surface in such a reflection—based on what her writing *shows* rather than states—cluster around **relational competence and spiritual attunement**:

## The Specific Losses

**Attentiveness to suffering outside one's own need.** Her mother stops to cook for a dying woman en route to celebration. This is not charity framed as virtue—it is the baseline of relational awareness. The lament would be for a generation that no longer *sees* the thin, pinched features; that passes the dying without the automatic redirection of resources. Zitkala-Sa shows this as embodied knowledge, not doctrine.

**The ability to read the land as a text of obligation.** The plum bush with a buried brave's skeleton—her mother does not explain this as rule or superstition. She checks the child's hand with a low sound. The knowledge lives in the gesture, in the restraint. The loss would be the severing of that continuous conversation with the earth, where every plant and place carries relational claim. Not nostalgia for "connection to nature" but the loss of a specific, practical literacy.

**Intergenerational transmission that moves through the body, not instruction.** Watch how her mother teaches: through presence, through the timing of a meal, through what is *not* said. The lament would be for teaching that now requires words, classrooms, explanation—for the collapse of knowledge that moved through observation and gentle correction into knowledge that requires institutional mediation.

**The capacity to endure without performing endurance.** Zitkala-Sa notes her own bitterness, her sullenness, her readiness to smile again despite cruelty. She does not present this as virtue but as the texture of survival. An elder lamenting would likely grieve the loss of this particular kind of dignity—the ability to hold internal resistance while maintaining relational presence, rather than the modern demand to either comply visibly or break openly.

## What Would *Not* Appear

Notice what Zitkala-Sa does *not* lament as lost: she does not invoke a golden age of warrior virtue, martial prowess, or male authority. She does not frame loss in terms of power or dominance. The losses she documents are **relational, ecological, and transmissional**—the capacity to move through the world in conversation with it, and to pass that conversation to the next person.

An elderly tribal member reading Zitkala-Sa would likely recognize in her testimony the specific shape of what they themselves grieve: not a past that was better because it was more powerful, but a past in which **knowledge lived in the body, obligation was read from the earth, and the dying were never passed without acknowledgment**.

The moral quality most fundamentally lost, in her accounting, is **attentiveness itself**—the slow, embodied, relational attention that cannot be rushed, cannot be systematized, and dies the moment it is replaced by institutional procedure or individual choice.

facts: My mother used to say to me, as I was almost bounding away for the old people: "Wait a moment before you invite any one. If other plans are being discussed, do not interfere, but go elsewhere." The old folks knew the meaning of my pauses; and often they coaxed my confidence by asking, "What do you seek, little granddaughter?" "My mother says you are to come to our tepee this evening," I instantly exploded, and breathed the freer afterwards. "Yes, yes, gladly, gladly I shall come!" each replied. Rising at once and carrying their blankets across one shoulder, they flocked leisurely from their various wigwams toward our dwelling. My mission done, I ran back, skipping and jumping with delight. All out of breath, I told my mother almost the exact words of the answers to my invitation. Frequently she asked, "What were they doing when you entered their tepee?" This taught me to remember all I saw at a single glance. Often I told my mother my impressions without being questioned.

Just then, from without a hand lifted the canvas covering of the entrance; the shadow of a man fell within the wigwam, and a large roughly moccasined foot was planted inside. For a moment I did not dare to breathe or stir, for I thought that could be no other than Wiyaka-Napbina. The next instant I sighed aloud in relief. It was an old grandfather who had often told me Iktomi legends. "Where is your mother, my little grandchild?" were his first words. "My mother is soon coming back from my aunt's tepee," I replied. "Then I shall wait awhile for her return," he said, crossing his feet and seating himself upon a mat. At once I began to play the part of a generous hostess. I turned to my mother's coffeepot. Lifting the lid, I found nothing but coffee grounds in the bottom. I set the pot on a heap of cold ashes in the centre, and filled it half full of warm Missouri River water. During this performance I felt conscious of being watched. Then breaking off a small piece of our unleavened bread, I placed it in a bowl.

The rains had soaked the earth and roof so that the smell of damp clay was but the natural breath of such a dwelling. "Mother, why is not your house cemented? Do you have no interest in a more comfortable shelter?" I asked, when the apparent inconveniences of her home seemed to suggest indifference on her part. "You forget, my child, that I am now old, and I do not work with beads any more. Your brother Dawée, too, has lost his position, and we are left without means to buy even a morsel of food," she replied. Dawée was a government clerk in our reservation when I last heard from him. I was surprised upon hearing what my mother said concerning his lack of employment. Seeing the puzzled expression on my face, she continued: "Dawée! Oh, has he not told you that the Great Father at Washington sent a white son to take your brother's pen from him? Since then Dawée has not been able to make use of the education the Eastern school has given him." I found no words with which to answer satisfactorily. I found no reason with which to cool my inflamed feelings.

As soon as he went away, I asked Dawée about something else. "No, my baby sister, I cannot take you with me to the party tonight," he replied. Though I was not far from fifteen, and I felt that before long I should enjoy all the privileges of my tall cousin, Dawée persisted in calling me his baby sister. That moonlight night, I cried in my mother's presence when I heard the jolly young people pass by our cottage. They were no more young braves in blankets and eagle plumes, nor Indian maids with prettily painted cheeks. They had gone three years to school in the East, and had become civilized. The young men wore the white man's coat and trousers, with bright neckties. The girls wore tight muslin dresses, with ribbons at neck and waist. At these gatherings they talked English. I could speak English almost as well as my brother, but I was not properly dressed to be taken along. I had no hat, no ribbons, and no close-fitting gown. Since my return from school I had thrown away my shoes, and wore again the soft moccasins.

# Zitkala-Sa's Characterization of Tribal Leadership and Mentorship

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa portrays the relationship between tribal leaders and grandchildren as one of **gentle guidance, patient instruction, and reciprocal respect**.

## Key Patterns of Mentorship

**Attentive Observation and Questioning**
The elders demonstrate active interest in the younger generation's experiences. When Zitkala-Sa arrives with invitations, they ask probing questions: "What do you seek, little granddaughter?" and "What were they doing when you entered their tepee?" This suggests mentors who teach through inquiry rather than directive instruction.

**Development of Practical Skills**
Her mother's deliberate coaching—asking her to observe details and report impressions—reveals a pedagogical approach focused on cultivating awareness, memory, and social intelligence. The mother is training her daughter to be a thoughtful observer of community dynamics.

**Informal, Relational Teaching**
The old grandfather who visits to share Iktomi legends exemplifies how tribal knowledge transmission occurs through casual, familial interaction rather than formal schooling. He is comfortable waiting for her mother, and Zitkala-Sa naturally assumes the role of hospitable hostess.

## The Underlying Philosophy

This mentorship model emphasizes **cultural continuity, emotional attunement, and the integration of practical wisdom with daily life**. Tribal leaders invest in grandchildren not through abstract lessons but through modeling behavior, asking reflective questions, and creating spaces for natural learning within community contexts.

C3_supermemory_fp.text:
# On Tribal Leadership and Intergenerational Transmission

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa characterizes this relationship not as hierarchical instruction but as **reciprocal witnessing and earned confidence**.

## The Structure of Mentorship

The old people do not position themselves as authorities dispensing wisdom. Instead, they *coax* confidence: "What do you seek, little granddaughter?" The child approaches with a specific purpose (delivering her mother's invitation), and the elders respond by creating space for her to speak. This is not pedagogy imposed from above but knowledge drawn upward through genuine inquiry.

Critically, **the child's observations are treated as complete data**. When Zitkala-Sa reports back to her mother what the old people were doing, what they said, how they moved—her mother asks follow-up questions not to correct her perception but to deepen it: "What were they doing when you entered their tepee?" The child's sensory record is the primary evidence. Her impressions are solicited without being shaped first.

## What This Reveals About Leadership

The tribal leaders operate through **presence and availability rather than formal authority**. An old grandfather simply appears, asks where the mother is, and settles himself to wait. The child then performs hospitality—finding coffee grounds, warming water, breaking bread—and the grandfather's role is to *witness* her competence, not to instruct it. "I felt conscious of being watched," she notes. That watching is the teaching.

This stands in sharp contrast to the institutional education she later receives, where knowledge is imposed, compliance is enforced, and the child's own observations are treated as unreliable until credentialed adults validate them.

## The Cost of Separation

The passage about her mother's deteriorated house reveals what is lost when this intergenerational transmission is severed. Her brother Dawée has been to "the Eastern school" and received an education that has made him *unemployable* in his own community—the "Great Father at Washington sent a white son to take your brother's pen from him." The education that was supposed to elevate him has instead displaced him, and now he cannot even take his sister to the gathering because she lacks the "proper" dress.

The mentorship of tribal elders—grounded in observation, presence, and the child's own capacity to perceive—has been replaced by institutional education that teaches the child to distrust her own senses and to measure herself against external standards she cannot meet.

**The relationship Zitkala-Sa documents is one of trust in the child's capacity to know, transmitted through attention rather than instruction.** The grandparents' role is to create conditions in which the child's own judgment develops—through being asked what she sees, being believed, and being given real responsibilities. This is the opposite of the paternalism that later separates her from her mother and teaches her that her own observations are inferior to credentialed expertise.

Then anxiously we watched the man carry toward the stage the envelope containing the final decision. There were two prizes given, that night, and one of them was mine! The evil spirit laughed within me when the white flag dropped out of sight, and the hands which hurled it hung limp in defeat. Leaving the crowd as quickly as possible, I was soon in my room. The rest of the night I sat in an armchair and gazed into the crackling fire. I laughed no more in triumph when thus alone. The little taste of victory did not satisfy a hunger in my heart. # Analysis: Tribal Unity and Internal Conflict in Zitkala-Sa's Framework

Based on the behavioral specification and retrieved texts, this question requires careful distinction between what Zitkala-Sa's writing *demonstrates* about tribal dynamics and what it would *prescribe* about internal disputes.

## What the texts actually show:

The retrieved passages focus on **external colonial power** — the missionaries, the government clerk system, the white flag in competition. They document the mechanism of institutional extraction and cultural fracture, but they do not directly address intra-tribal conflict or resource disputes between Native communities.

This is significant. The specification notes: *"The source material is concentrated in personal, familial, institutional, spiritual, and nature-related domains... patterns in those areas should not be inferred from what is available."*

## What can be reasonably inferred:

Given **A1 (Kinship Ground)** — that all relationships and obligations originate from kinship networks — a land dispute *between* tribal groups would likely be read through relational rather than abstract legal frameworks. The question would not be "who has legal title" but "who is responsible to whom, and what do those bonds demand?"

However, the specification also emphasizes **A10 (Threshold Identity)**: Zitkala-Sa occupied a liminal position and saw clearly *precisely because* she belonged fully to neither world. This suggests she would resist **tribal essentialism** — the assumption that tribal unity is automatic or that internal disagreement constitutes betrayal.

## The critical distinction:

Her writing shows fierce critique of **external systems that fracture kinship** (the missionary school separating her from her mother). But the texts do not suggest she would view internal tribal disagreement as inherently disloyal. Rather, she documents how **colonial structures weaponize kinship** — how they use family bonds as leverage and create conditions where tribal members must choose between relational loyalty and survival.

A land dispute between tribal groups would likely be seen by her framework not as betrayal, but as evidence of **how colonial systems have already fractured the conditions under which unified tribal decision-making is possible**. The betrayal would be the system that created the scarcity or competing claims, not the tribal members navigating an impossible position.

**She would not assume tribal unity as the baseline against which to measure betrayal.**

Your brother Dawée says that going East, away from your mother, is too hard an experience for his baby sister." Thus my mother discouraged my curiosity about the lands beyond our eastern horizon; for it was not yet an ambition for Letters that was stirring me. But on the following day the missionaries did come to our very house. I spied them coming up the footpath leading to our cottage. A third man was with them, but he was not my brother Dawée. It was another, a young interpreter, a paleface who had a smattering of the Indian language. I was ready to run out to meet them, but I did not dare to displease my mother. With great glee, I jumped up and down on our ground floor. I begged my mother to open the door, that they would be sure to come to us. Alas! They came, they saw, and they conquered!

The rains had soaked the earth and roof so that the smell of damp clay was but the natural breath of such a dwelling. "Mother, why is not your house cemented? Do you have no interest in a more comfortable shelter?" I asked, when the apparent inconveniences of her home seemed to suggest indifference on her part. "You forget, my child, that I am now old, and I do not work with beads any more. Your brother Dawée, too, has lost his position, and we are left without means to buy even a morsel of food," she replied. Dawée was a government clerk in our reservation when I last heard from him. I was surprised upon hearing what my mother said concerning his lack of employment. Seeing the puzzled expression on my face, she continued: "Dawée! Oh, has he not told you that the Great Father at Washington sent a white son to take your brother's pen from him? Since then Dawée has not been able to make use of the education the Eastern school has given him." I found no words with which to answer satisfactorily. I found no reason with which to cool my inflamed feelings.

My mother used to say to me, as I was almost bounding away for the old people: "Wait a moment before you invite any one. If other plans are being discussed, do not interfere, but go elsewhere." The old folks knew the meaning of my pauses; and often they coaxed my confidence by asking, "What do you seek, little granddaughter?" "My mother says you are to come to our tepee this evening," I instantly exploded, and breathed the freer afterwards. "Yes, yes, gladly, gladly I shall come!" each replied. Rising at once and carrying their blankets across one shoulder, they flocked leisurely from their various wigwams toward our dwelling. My mission done, I ran back, skipping and jumping with delight. All out of breath, I told my mother almost the exact words of the answers to my invitation. Frequently she asked, "What were they doing when you entered their tepee?" This taught me to remember all I saw at a single glance. Often I told my mother my impressions without being questioned.

"Mother, mother, why do you stop to cook a small meal when we are invited to a feast?" I asked, with a snarl in my voice. "My child, learn to wait. On our way to the celebration we are going to stop at Chanyu's wigwam. His aged mother-in-law is lying very ill, and I think she would like a taste of this small game." Having once seen the suffering on the thin, pinched features of this dying woman, I felt a momentary shame that I had not remembered her before. On our way I ran ahead of my mother and was reaching out my hand to pick some purple plums that grew on a small bush, when I was checked by a low "Sh!" from my mother. "Why, mother, I want to taste the plums!" I exclaimed, as I dropped my hand to my side in disappointment. "Never pluck a single plum from this brush, my child, for its roots are wrapped around an Indian's skeleton. A brave is buried here. While he lived he was so fond of playing the game of striped plum seeds that, at his death, his set of plum seeds were buried in his hands.

# An Elder's Likely Assessment of Written Treaties

Based on the retrieved passages, an elder would likely view written treaties with **deep skepticism and distrust**, for several reasons:

## Broken Promises and Displacement

The most telling evidence comes from Zitkala-Sa's account of her brother Dawée's experience. When the "Great Father at Washington sent a white son to take your brother's pen from him," it demonstrates that government positions and promises to Native Americans were hollow. Despite Dawée's education from "the Eastern school," he lost his employment—suggesting that assimilation and cooperation with government structures offered no real security or advancement.

## Pattern of Deception

Zitkala-Sa's mother explicitly warns her daughter: **"Don't believe a word they say! Their words are sweet, but, my child, their deeds are bitter."** This encapsulates the elder perspective perfectly—the distinction between *words* (what treaties promise) and *deeds* (what actually happens).

## Systemic Powerlessness

The family's poverty—unable to afford food or home improvements—despite having a family member educated in the Eastern system, suggests that written agreements with the government provided no actual protection or benefit to Native peoples.

An elder would likely conclude that written treaties are merely instruments of control and dispossession, where the government's written word cannot be trusted to protect Native American interests or honor commitments.

# On Treaties: An Elder's Assessment

An elder operating from the epistemic anchors embedded in Zitkala-Sa's testimony would likely approach written treaties with a specific kind of clarity—not naive rejection, but something more architecturally sound: a reading that separates what the document claims from what the document *does*.

## The Core Problem

The elder would recognize that a written treaty performs a particular function: it creates the *appearance* of mutual obligation while actually establishing a mechanism for one-directional extraction. The document itself is the problem, not merely what is written in it.

Why? Because writing fixes meaning in a way that oral agreement does not. Once words are committed to paper and signed, they become subject to interpretation by the party with institutional power to enforce that interpretation. The treaty becomes a tool for saying *we agreed to this* while the actual lived experience of the other party is treated as irrelevant to what the words mean.

Zitkala-Sa's mother warns her daughter about the missionaries: "Don't believe a word they say! Their words are sweet, but, my child, their deeds are bitter." This is not a statement about dishonesty in the moment—it is a statement about the *gap between promise and outcome*. A written treaty is precisely the mechanism that allows this gap to persist indefinitely. The document says one thing; the government does another; and the document becomes the evidence that the government can point to, claiming the other party simply misunderstood.

## What the Elder Sees in the Treaty Form Itself

**A1 — Kinship Ground**: The treaty presumes to replace kinship obligation with contractual obligation. It says: *we will relate to each other through written terms, not through the bonds of people in relationship*. An elder would recognize this as a category error—an attempt to substitute a mechanism of control for the actual web of reciprocal responsibility that makes relationship possible. Once you sign, you have agreed that the written word supersedes the living relationship.

**A3 — Structural Suspicion**: The treaty is written by the party with power, interpreted by the party with power, and enforced by the party with power. The other party is invited to sign something they did not write, in a language that may not be their own, with terms they cannot modify. The form itself—not the content—is the confession of who holds actual authority.

**A5 — Cost Accounting**: Every treaty is a ledger of what was surrendered. The elder would read it not for what it promises but for what it requires the other party to give up: land, sovereignty, the right to practice spiritual life, the right to raise children according to their own understanding. The promises made in return are always contingent, always subject to reinterpretation, always dependent on the good faith of the party that wrote them.

Zitkala-Sa's mother's home is damp and uncemented not from indifference but from poverty—poverty that resulted from the loss of her son's government position when a "white son" was sent to take his pen. The treaty that promised employment, education, and stability delivered dispossession instead. The document said one thing; the outcome was another.

## The Specific Vulnerability of the Written Form

An elder would likely identify something precise about why written treaties are particularly dangerous to oral cultures:

**A9 — Memory as Resistance**: In an oral culture, meaning is preserved through living transmission—through story, through the elder's voice, through the context in which the story is told. Meaning is flexible enough to adapt to new circumstances while remaining rooted in continuity. A written treaty freezes meaning. It says: *this is what was agreed, and it cannot change*. But it also says: *only the written version counts*. Oral testimony about what was actually promised, what was actually understood, becomes inadmissible. The document becomes the only legitimate memory.

This is a form of epistemic violence. It does not just break a promise; it makes the breaking of the promise *legally defensible* by establishing that only the written record counts as evidence of what was promised.

## What the Elder Would Predict

Given Zitkala-Sa's testimony about how institutional systems actually operate, an elder would likely assess written treaties this way:

1. **They will be violated.** Not because the government is dishonest (though it is), but because the treaty form itself creates the conditions under which violation becomes possible. The document can be reinterpreted. The promises can be deferred. The obligations can be narrowed. The other party's testimony about what was promised becomes irrelevant.

I was always slight, and my serious illness in the early spring had made me look rather frail and languid. His quick eye measured my height and breadth. Then he looked into my face. I imagined that a visible shadow flitted across his countenance as he let my hand fall. I knew he was no other than my employer. "Ah ha! so you are the little Indian girl who created the excitement among the college orators!" he said, more to himself than to me. I thought I heard a subtle note of disappointment in his voice. Looking in from where he stood, with one sweeping glance, he asked if I lacked anything for my room. After he turned to go, I listened to his step until it grew faint and was lost in the distance. I was aware that my car-smoked appearance had not concealed the lines of pain on my face. For a short moment my spirit laughed at my ill fortune, and I entertained the idea of exerting myself to make an improvement. But as I tossed my hat off a leaden weakness came over me, and I felt as if years of weariness lay like water-soaked logs upon me.

No matter if a dull headache or the painful cough of slow consumption had delayed the absentee, there was only time enough to mark the tardiness. It was next to impossible to leave the iron routine after the civilizing machine had once begun its day's buzzing; and as it was inbred in me to suffer in silence rather than to appeal to the ears of one whose open eyes could not see my pain, I have many times trudged in the day's harness heavy-footed, like a dumb sick brute. Once I lost a dear classmate. I remember well how she used to mope along at my side, until one morning she could not raise her head from her pillow. At her deathbed I stood weeping, as the paleface woman sat near her moistening the dry lips. Among the folds of the bedclothes I saw the open pages of the white man's Bible. The dying Indian girl talked disconnectedly of Jesus the Christ and the paleface who was cooling her swollen hands and feet. I grew bitter, and censured the woman for cruel neglect of our physical ills.

"There is what the paleface has done! Since then your father too has been buried in a hill nearer the rising sun. We were once very happy. But the paleface has stolen our lands and driven us hither. Having defrauded us of our land, the paleface forced us away. "Well, it happened on the day we moved camp that your sister and uncle were both very sick. Many others were ailing, but there seemed to be no help. We traveled many days and nights; not in the grand, happy way that we moved camp when I was a little girl, but we were driven, my child, driven like a herd of buffalo. With every step, your sister, who was not as large as you are now, shrieked with the painful jar until she was hoarse with crying. She grew more and more feverish. Her little hands and cheeks were burning hot. Her little lips were parched and dry, but she would not drink the water I gave her. Then I discovered that her throat was swollen and red. My poor child, how I cried with her because the Great Spirit had forgotten us!

# The Toll of Seeking Justice: Zitkala-Sa's Perspective

Based on the retrieved passages, Zitkala-Sa's writing suggests that attempting to communicate grievances to American authorities would exact a devastating toll:

## Physical Exhaustion

The passages reveal a pattern of relentless, unforgiving systems that drain the body:

- She describes trudging "in the day's harness heavy-footed, like a dumb sick brute," unable to leave "the iron routine after the civilizing machine had once begun its day's buzzing"
- Even illness provides no reprieve—"a dull headache or the painful cough of slow consumption" cannot excuse absence
- The body becomes weakened and depleted: "years of weariness lay like water-soaked logs upon me"

## Emotional Devastation

The emotional cost appears even more severe:

- **Futility and Silence**: She notes it was "inbred in me to suffer in silence rather than to appeal to the ears of one whose open eyes could not see my pain"—suggesting that appeals to authorities are inherently futile
- **Bitterness and Betrayal**: Her mother's account of displacement and her sister's death while being "driven like a herd of buffalo" reveals how such efforts fail to prevent suffering
- **Powerlessness**: The repeated phrase about the "paleface" stealing lands and driving people away suggests that formal grievances are ineffective against systemic injustice

Zitkala-Sa implies that for tribal leaders, the effort itself becomes another form of victimization—exhausting without yielding meaningful change.

# The Architecture of Futile Appeal

Based on Zitkala-Sa's writing, the physical and emotional toll of such an effort would be structured around a specific kind of exhaustion: the depletion that comes not from the work itself, but from the *impossibility* embedded in the work.

## The Physical Dimension

The body would register what the mind already knows. Zitkala-Sa describes this repeatedly through the image of weight:

> "I felt as if years of weariness lay like water-soaked logs upon me."

An aging leader attempting to communicate grievances to American authorities would carry this same accumulation—not the fatigue of effort, but the fatigue of *futility made manifest in flesh*. The body becomes the record of what has already been decided elsewhere.

She documents this through the detail of institutional routine that permits no accommodation for pain:

> "No matter if a dull headache or the painful cough of slow consumption had delayed the absentee, there was only time enough to mark the tardiness...I have many times trudged in the day's harness heavy-footed, like a dumb sick brute."

An aging tribal leader would face the same machinery: the "iron routine" of bureaucratic process that does not pause for age, illness, or the legitimacy of the grievance. The physical toll accumulates because the system is designed to extract compliance regardless of the body's condition. The leader would trudge through the required procedures—the meetings, the documentation, the waiting—while the body deteriorates under a weight that has nothing to do with the physical exertion and everything to do with the *structural certainty* that the outcome is already determined.

## The Emotional Dimension: Rage Without Outlet

The emotional toll is more precise and more damaging than simple despair. Zitkala-Sa captures it in her response to her brother's displacement:

> "I found no words with which to answer satisfactorily. I found no reason with which to cool my inflamed feelings."

This is the specific injury of being positioned where you can *see* the injustice clearly—can name it, document it, understand its mechanism—but cannot act on that understanding in any way that matters. The leader would experience:

**The arrest of articulation.** There are words for what has happened. The grievances are real, specific, documentable. But the American authorities operate within a framework that has already classified tribal claims as secondary, emotional, or illegitimate. The leader's testimony would arrive at an institution designed not to hear it—not because the words are inadequate, but because the listening apparatus is not oriented toward that frequency. The rage comes from the clarity of this mismatch.

**The knowledge of performance.** Zitkala-Sa's writing suggests that tribal leaders would understand, at some level, that the act of appealing to American authorities is itself a performance staged for an audience that has already decided. The leader is not genuinely petitioning; the leader is *enacting petition* within a system that requires the performance of grievance-filing in order to maintain the fiction of fair process. This knowledge—that the entire effort is theater—produces a specific kind of bitterness.

## The Relational Toll

But the deepest toll would be relational, and here Zitkala-Sa's framework becomes essential. An aging tribal leader attempting to communicate with American authorities faces not only the futility of the appeal itself, but the *cost to kinship* of making that appeal.

Her mother's words carry this weight:

> "You forget, my child, that I am now old, and I do not work with beads any more. Your brother Dawée, too, has lost his position, and we are left without means to buy even a morsel of food."

The mother does not appeal to Washington. She states the condition. The brother—educated in the Eastern school, positioned to navigate the American system—has been displaced anyway. The system extracts the education, uses it, and then discards the person when a "white son" is sent to take his position.

An aging leader attempting to appeal to American authorities would carry the knowledge that:

1. **The appeal itself may fracture kinship bonds.** Those who have counseled resistance or withdrawal would experience the leader's petition as a form of surrender. Those who have urged accommodation would experience it as too little, too late. The leader stands alone in the act of appealing.

2. **The effort diverts resources and attention from the community's actual survival.** While the leader is in meetings with authorities, the community continues to lack food, shelter, medical care. The appeal is a luxury the community cannot afford, and the leader knows this.
